proposals.title: Automated Force Field Parameterization for Inorganic and Organometallic Drug-like Molecules Using the SILCS Methodology
proposals.motivation: Inorganic and organometallic complexes are emerging as promising drug candidates, offering unique geometries, coordination chemistries, and mechanisms of action distinct from purely organic molecules. However, their computational drug design is hindered by the lack of automated, accurate, and transferable force field parameters, which are critical for structure-based methods like Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS). Pre-2026, parameter assignment for these species is often manual, system-specific, and a major bottleneck.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the automated topology and parameter assignment algorithms developed within the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) and SILCS framework can be systematically extended to handle the unique bonding, metal coordination, and electronic features of inorganic and organometallic drug-like molecules, enabling their seamless integration into SILCS simulations for binding site identification and affinity prediction.
proposals.method: We will extend the existing CGenFF program and SILCS-Biologics parameter assignment algorithms to recognize common inorganic/organometallic motifs (e.g., metallocenes, platinum complexes, metal-carbonyls). The approach will involve: 1) curating a training set of quantum mechanical (QM) data (geometries, vibrational frequencies, torsional profiles) for representative metal-ligand fragments; 2) developing new atom types and validation rules for metal centers and coordinating atoms; 3) implementing assignment logic for coordination bonds and formal charges within the automated workflow; and 4) validating parameters by calculating hydration free energies (via SILCS) and comparing to experimental data or high-level QM for test molecules.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first automated, general-purpose force field parameterization tool tailored for inorganic and organometallic drug discovery within the widely used CHARMM/SILCS ecosystem. It will directly enable SILCS-based virtual screening and binding site mapping for this important but underexplored class of therapeutics, accelerating their rational design.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS automation has been applied to proteins, biologics, and organic ligands, its extension to handle the complex bonding and electronic environments of metal-containing drug-like molecules is a novel and non-trivial advancement, moving beyond the organic-centric paradigm of pre-2026 automated force field tools.

proposals.title: Large-Scale Benchmarking of Automated Parameter and Charge Assignment for Drug-Like Molecules Using the SILCS Platform
proposals.motivation: The accuracy of molecular mechanics force fields, particularly partial atomic charges and Lennard-Jones parameters, is critical for reliable structure-based drug design (SBDD) and binding affinity prediction. While the SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform automates parameter assignment via its Fragment-Based Drug Design (FBDD) and SILCS-Biologics workflows, its performance has not been systematically validated across the vast chemical space of modern drug discovery libraries. A comprehensive, large-scale benchmark is needed to establish confidence in these automated methods for diverse, novel chemotypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the automated parameter and charge assignment protocols within the SILCS framework will demonstrate high accuracy and transferability across a broad range of drug-like molecules when benchmarked against high-level quantum mechanical (QM) calculations and experimental physicochemical data.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark set of 5,000+ molecules from diverse libraries (e.g., ChEMBL, ZINC, FDA-approved drugs). For each molecule, we will compute reference partial charges and conformational energies using high-level QM methods (MP2/cc-pVTZ). These will be compared against charges and parameters generated automatically by the SILCS-Biologics and FBDD workflows. We will assess accuracy using metrics like root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) for charges and relative conformational energies. The analysis will be extended to evaluate the impact of assigned parameters on the prediction of hydration free energies (via SILCS-MC) and protein-ligand binding poses/affinities for a subset of targets with known structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, publicly available benchmark for automated force field parameter assignment within a widely used SBDD platform. It will establish clear error bounds and best-practice guidelines for SILCS users, directly enhancing the reliability of covalent and non-covalent drug design projects. The resulting dataset will serve as a community resource for future force field development and validation.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS applications are well-published, a systematic, large-scale validation of its core parameterization engine is lacking. This proposal moves beyond case studies to a rigorous, statistical evaluation of accuracy across chemical space, which is non-trivial given the complexity of integrating fragment-based sampling, QM-derived libraries, and bio-molecular force fields.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Virtual Screening via Optimized SILCS-Based Parameterization of Ultra-Large Chemical Libraries
proposals.motivation: Virtual screening of ultra-large compound libraries (>100 million molecules) is a critical frontier in drug discovery, but it is bottlenecked by the computational cost of generating accurate force field parameters for novel chemical matter. The Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology provides a robust, physics-based framework for ligand parameterization and binding site identification, but its current assignment algorithms are not optimized for the scale required for high-throughput screening. Accelerating this step is essential to leverage the full potential of emerging billion-molecule libraries for rapid hit identification.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by implementing graph-based hashing and optimized spatial partitioning algorithms for the SILCS FragMaps, we can drastically reduce the computational cost of ligand functional group assignment and parameter derivation, enabling the screening of ultra-large libraries with SILCS accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop and benchmark a new computational workflow. First, we will replace the current exhaustive grid search for FragMap-ligand atom assignment with a k-dimensional tree (kd-tree) spatial data structure for rapid nearest-neighbor queries of FragMap grid points. Second, we will implement a graph-based hashing algorithm to create unique, pre-computed fingerprints for common chemical functional groups and their SILCS parameter sets, allowing for instant retrieval instead of on-the-fly calculation. The optimized pipeline will be integrated into the existing SILCS-Biologics framework and validated by re-screening known actives and decoys from the DUD-E and DEKOIS 2.0 benchmarks, comparing speed and accuracy against the standard protocol.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce an open-source, scalable extension of the SILCS platform capable of processing millions of compounds per day on standard computing clusters. It will directly enable structure-based virtual screening at an unprecedented scale while maintaining the methodological rigor of the SILCS approach. The resulting tool will be a significant contribution to the computational drug discovery community, bridging the gap between detailed binding site mapping and large-scale library enumeration.
proposals.novelty: While other groups have focused on machine learning for direct affinity prediction, this work uniquely optimizes the foundational physics-based parameterization step within the established SILCS framework, preserving its strengths in explicit solvation and binding site plasticity while overcoming its primary throughput limitation. The integration of graph hashing for functional group parameters is a novel acceleration strategy specific to the SILCS methodology.

proposals.title: Inverse SILCS: A Generative Framework for De Novo Ligand Design by Optimizing Free Energy Profiles in Target Binding Sites
proposals.motivation: Current structure-based drug design often relies on screening pre-existing libraries, which limits chemical novelty and exploration. The SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform provides detailed, all-atom free energy (GFE) maps that quantify favorable and unfavorable interactions for molecular fragments at a protein binding site. However, the process of translating these optimal interaction fields back into novel, synthetically accessible chemical entities remains a manual, intuition-driven challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by treating SILCS GFE maps as a target objective function, a generative molecular design algorithm can be trained to propose novel chemical structures that minimize the total penalty score (i.e., maximize predicted binding affinity) for a specific protein target.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional generative model, specifically a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based variational autoencoder (VAE) or a transformer architecture, trained on large chemical libraries (e.g., ZINC). The model's latent space will be conditioned on target-specific SILCS GFE maps, encoded as 3D voxel grids. The training objective will combine standard reconstruction loss with a physics-informed loss term derived from the SILCS scoring function, forcing the decoder to generate molecules whose computed fragment poses align with favorable GFE regions. The framework will be integrated with the existing SILCS-Biologics and SILCS-Covalent workflows for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a closed-loop, computational pipeline from protein structure to novel lead-like molecules, directly bridging free energy landscapes with generative chemistry. It will extend the utility of the SILCS platform from analysis and scoring to proactive design, significantly accelerating the early hit identification phase. The method will be validated by generating de novo inhibitors for targets with known SILCS maps, such as ER α-glucosidase I.
proposals.novelty: While generative models for molecules exist, conditioning them directly on experimentally-validated, all-atom free energy fields from the SILCS methodology is a novel integration. This moves beyond simple pharmacophore matching or docking scores to optimize for a comprehensive, physics-based interaction potential from the outset of design.

proposals.title: Mapping Hydration Pathways and Hydrogen-Bond Networks for Charged Residues in Lipid Bilayers Using SILCS-Based Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The hydration of charged protein residues within or near lipid bilayers is critical for membrane protein function, stability, and ligand binding, yet the detailed pathways and energetics of water penetration remain poorly characterized. A predictive, atomic-level understanding of these hydration networks is essential for rational drug design targeting membrane proteins, where water-mediated interactions often govern binding affinity and specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, by explicitly mapping the free energy of water and functional groups, can reveal distinct, reproducible pathways and hydrogen-bonding networks that facilitate the solvation of charged residues within the heterogeneous membrane environment.
proposals.method: We will employ all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of model lipid bilayers (e.g., POPC) containing single charged amino acids (e.g., Arg, Lys, Asp). The SILCS-Biologics approach will be used to compute 3D free energy maps (FragMaps) for water and chemical probes representing hydrogen bond donors/acceptors. Enhanced sampling techniques, such as Hamiltonian replica exchange, will be integrated to improve sampling of water penetration events. Analysis will focus on water density, residence times, and the connectivity of hydrogen-bond networks linking the bulk solvent to the buried residue.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, energetically grounded map of membrane hydration pathways, directly linking water structure to the stability of charged protein motifs. It will deliver a computational framework for predicting hydration sites in membrane-bound drug targets, thereby enhancing structure-based drug design for GPCRs, ion channels, and other membrane proteins.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been applied to soluble proteins and ligand design, its application to explicitly map the thermodynamics and kinetics of water pathways in membranes for charged residue solvation is novel. This integrates the SILCS-Biologics framework with membrane biophysics to solve a long-standing problem in hydration energetics.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Lipid Sensing Response: A SILCS-Biologics Study of Membrane Protein-Induced Lipid Conformational and Dynamic Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Membrane protein function is critically modulated by the surrounding lipid environment, yet the reciprocal influence—how a protein alters the conformational landscape of its annular and bulk lipids—remains poorly quantified. Understanding this 'inverse problem' is essential for deciphering lipid-mediated signaling, protein stabilization, and drug binding in membranes. Pre-2026 methods largely treat lipids as a passive matrix, creating a knowledge gap in the dynamic protein-lipid interplay.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedded membrane proteins act as allosteric sensors, inducing specific, quantifiable changes in the conformational preferences, dynamics, and headgroup orientation of surrounding phospholipids, which can be captured and predicted using ensemble-based computational saturation methods.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, extending the SILCS-Biologics framework to model complex lipid mixtures. Molecular dynamics simulations with the CHARMM36 force field will be used to generate conformational ensembles for lipid-only and protein-embedded systems. SILCS will then be applied to compute 3D site interaction fields (FragMaps) for lipid headgroups and tails, enabling a direct, atomistic comparison of lipid conformational thermodynamics and dynamics in the presence versus absence of specific membrane protein targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first systematic, physics-based map of protein-induced lipid adaptation, moving beyond static lipid binding sites to a dynamic sensing model. It will provide a novel computational workflow to predict how protein mutations or drug binding alter the lipid sensing response, offering new insights for designing membrane protein stabilizers or allosteric modulators.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been used for protein-centric drug and biologics design, its application to solve the inverse problem—treating the protein as the fixed perturbation to map lipid conformational response—is a novel inversion of the standard paradigm. This shifts the focus from 'protein sensing lipids' to 'lipids sensing protein'.

proposals.title: Solvent-Aware Optimization of Backbone Torsion Parameters for the CHARMM Force Field Using SILCS-Guided Knowledge-Based Potentials
proposals.motivation: Accurate backbone conformational sampling is critical for protein-ligand binding predictions and structure-based drug design. Current force field parameterization, including the CHARMM force field, often relies on gas-phase quantum mechanics or implicit solvent models, which can inadequately capture the explicit, directional interactions of water that critically influence backbone preferences. This limitation can propagate errors into SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) simulations, which depend on accurate solvation free energies for mapping functional binding sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimizing backbone torsion parameters using a knowledge-based potential derived from high-resolution protein crystal structures, while explicitly accounting for solvent occupancy patterns from SILCS simulations, will yield force field parameters that improve the prediction of protein conformational ensembles and ligand-binding site thermodynamics.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS-Biologics platform to perform all-atom, explicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations of a diverse set of protein structures. From these simulations, we will extract three-dimensional solvent occupancy maps (FragMaps) to define a solvent-inclusive knowledge-based potential. This potential will be integrated into an iterative optimization workflow targeting the backbone φ/ψ torsion parameters of the CHARMM force field. The optimization will minimize the difference between MD-sampled and crystallographically observed backbone dihedral distributions, with the penalty function weighted by the explicit solvent occupancy data to prioritize solvent-exposed and functionally relevant regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a revised set of CHARMM backbone torsion parameters with enhanced transferability for modeling proteins in aqueous environments. It will directly improve the physical realism of SILCS simulations for cryptic site prediction and covalent drug design. The integrated method establishes a novel framework for force field refinement that systematically incorporates explicit solvation effects from the outset.
proposals.novelty: While knowledge-based potentials exist, their direct integration with explicit-solvent SILCS FragMaps to guide force field torsion optimization is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond standard vacuum or implicit-solvent reference data, directly embedding the influence of explicit water structure into the parameterization process.

proposals.title: Derivation of Amino-Acid-Specific Solvation and Conformational Parameters from Ultra-High-Resolution Data on Dipeptide and Tripeptide Fragments for the CHARMM Force Field
proposals.motivation: Current biomolecular force fields, including CHARMM, rely on parameters derived from a limited set of model compounds, which may not fully capture the nuanced, context-dependent behavior of individual amino acids in proteins. This can lead to inaccuracies in predicting protein-ligand binding, conformational dynamics, and stability, directly impacting the reliability of structure-based drug design. Ultra-high-resolution crystallographic and NMR data on minimal peptide systems now provide an unprecedented opportunity to refine these fundamental parameters.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that amino-acid-specific solvation, torsional, and nonbonded parameters derived from fitting to ultra-high-resolution experimental data on dipeptide and tripeptide fragments will yield a more accurate and transferable CHARMM force field, leading to improved prediction of protein structure, dynamics, and ligand binding affinities.
proposals.method: We will apply the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology to dipeptide and tripeptide systems in explicit solvent. SILCS Monte Carlo simulations with explicit small molecule probes (water, benzene, etc.) will generate 3D maps of functional group binding affinities around each amino acid fragment. These maps, along with ultra-high-resolution conformational data from the Cambridge Structural Database and protein data bank, will serve as target data for iterative force field optimization using the Force Field Toolkit (ffTK) in CHARMM. Parameters for backbone and sidechain torsions, partial atomic charges, and Lennard-Jones radii will be systematically adjusted to minimize the difference between simulation and experiment.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a next-generation, amino-acid-parameterized dataset for the CHARMM force field, directly enhancing the accuracy of SILCS-based binding site identification and ligand design. It will provide a foundational improvement for computational drug discovery by enabling more reliable predictions of protein-ligand interactions and conformational ensembles.
proposals.novelty: While force field optimization is common, this approach uniquely leverages the SILCS-Biologics framework—typically used for proteins—to inform parameterization at the minimal fragment level, creating a direct link between fragment-level solvation properties and macromolecular modeling accuracy. The systematic use of ultra-high-resolution fragment data for amino-acid-specific parameter derivation represents a targeted advance beyond generic residue types.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of CHARMM36-Compatible Parameters for Glycolipids and Sterols to Enable SILCS-Based Analysis of Membrane-Protein-Ligand Interactions
proposals.motivation: The CHARMM36 lipid force field is a cornerstone for membrane simulations but lacks robust, validated parameters for major lipid classes like glycolipids (e.g., GM1) and sterols (e.g., cholesterol). This gap critically limits the application of advanced computational methods, such as Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS), to study membrane-associated drug targets where these lipids modulate protein structure, function, and ligand binding. Pre-2026, the increasing focus on membrane protein drug discovery necessitates accurate models of complex, biologically relevant membranes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by developing and rigorously validating CHARMM36-compatible parameters for key glycolipids and sterols, we will enable accurate SILCS simulations of heterogeneous membranes, leading to the identification of novel lipid-influenced binding sites and improved structure-based design for membrane protein targets.
proposals.method: We will employ the established CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) program and quantum mechanical (QM) target data for parameter derivation. For glycolipids, we will extend existing carbohydrate and lipid parameters, focusing on the glycosidic linkage and ceramide moiety. For cholesterol, we will refine torsional and nonbonded parameters using QM data on conformational energetics and crystal lattice properties. Validation will involve molecular dynamics simulations of pure lipid bilayers and mixed membranes, comparing calculated properties (area per lipid, order parameters, electron density profiles) to available experimental data. The final parameters will be integrated into the SILCS-Biologics framework for membrane-based binding site mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a critical, missing extension to the CHARMM36 force field, enabling realistic simulations of glycolipid- and cholesterol-rich membranes. It will directly enhance the SILCS platform's capability for membrane protein drug discovery by allowing competitive saturation simulations with these lipids, leading to more accurate identification of functional sites and design of ligands that account for lipid-mediated allostery.
proposals.novelty: While lipid parameters exist, a systematic, CHARMM36-compatible parameter set for complex glycolipids validated for use with the SILCS methodology is novel. The integration of these specific lipid classes into the SILCS-Biologics workflow for co-solvent mapping in membrane environments represents a significant methodological advance over existing site identification techniques.

proposals.title: Adversarial Sampling and Active Learning for the Closed-Loop Refinement of CHARMM Force Fields under Ionic Stress
proposals.motivation: Classical force fields, including the CHARMM family, are known to exhibit systematic errors in modeling ion-specific effects (Hofmeister series) and ion-pair interactions, particularly at non-physiological concentrations relevant to industrial formulations and extreme biological conditions. Current parameterization is largely manual and reactive, relying on error discovery after the fact, which limits the accuracy and transferability of simulations for drug discovery in complex electrolyte environments. A proactive, data-driven framework is needed to predict and preemptively correct these failures.
proposals.hypothesis: That a machine learning model trained on a curated dataset of force field errors across ionic conditions can accurately predict regions of parameter space prone to failure, and that using these predictions to guide targeted quantum mechanical calculations will create a self-improving force field with enhanced robustness for novel electrolyte solutions.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark dataset of simulation errors (vs. experimental data and CCSD(T)/MP2 references) for ion-solute and ion-pair interactions across varied salts and concentrations. A graph neural network (GNN) model will be trained on molecular fingerprints and environmental descriptors to predict error magnitudes. This model will act as an 'adversarial sampler,' identifying underperforming interaction motifs. Targeted QM calculations (at the DFT and MP2 levels) on these flagged motifs will feed into an automated parameter optimization workflow using the Force Field Toolkit (ffTK) and SILCS-based competitive binding profiles for validation, creating an iterative refinement loop.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, adaptive parameterization pipeline that moves force field development from passive validation to active error prediction and correction. It will produce a publicly available, iteratively refined CHARMM-compatible parameter set for ions and co-solutes with documented accuracy across a broad concentration range. The methodology establishes a paradigm for data-driven force field maintenance applicable to other challenging chemical spaces.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its use of an error-predicting ML model as an adversarial guide for resource allocation in force field refinement, creating a closed-loop, self-improving system. It directly integrates SILCS-based binding site analysis for functional validation within the parameter optimization cycle, a synergy not yet explored.

proposals.title: Bayesian Optimization for Automated, Data-Driven Refinement of CHARMM Force Field Parameters
proposals.motivation: The accuracy of molecular mechanics force fields, such as CHARMM, is foundational for reliable computer-aided drug design and biomolecular simulation. Current parameterization relies heavily on expert intuition and iterative manual adjustment against quantum mechanical (QM) and experimental target data, a process that is slow, labor-intensive, and can lead to suboptimal local minima. An automated, efficient, and systematic optimization framework is critically needed to accelerate the development of next-generation force fields with improved predictive power.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Bayesian optimization (BO), a machine learning strategy for global optimization of expensive black-box functions, can be adapted to efficiently and autonomously navigate the high-dimensional parameter space of a molecular mechanics force field, leading to parameter sets with superior agreement to target data compared to those derived from traditional manual protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop optimization pipeline where the objective function is the weighted error of molecular dynamics simulations against target data (e.g., QM conformational energies, liquid densities, diffusion coefficients). A Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate model will be trained on initial parameter sets and their corresponding error scores. Using an acquisition function (Expected Improvement), the BO algorithm will iteratively propose the next most informative parameter set to simulate, balancing exploration and exploitation. This loop will be integrated with the SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform for simulation execution and analysis, focusing initially on torsional and Lennard-Jones parameters for drug-like fragments.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated software module for the CHARMM/SILCS ecosystem that significantly reduces the human effort and computational cost of force field refinement. It will produce a proof-of-concept, optimized parameter set for a congeneric series of molecules, demonstrating improved predictive accuracy. The methodology establishes a generalizable paradigm for data-driven force field parameterization.
proposals.novelty: While BO is established for hyperparameter tuning in machine learning, its application to the direct, global optimization of physics-based force field parameters is novel. This approach moves beyond local gradient-based methods or grid searches, offering a principled framework to escape local minima and systematically incorporate diverse target data types.

proposals.title: Bayesian Calibration of Lipid Force Fields to Resolve Dynamic Stoichiometric Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of lipid complexes, such as micelles, bicelles, or lipid-protein assemblies, often rely on force fields (FFs) parameterized against bulk properties, which may not accurately capture the delicate free-energy balance between different stoichiometries. Pre-2026, the CHARMM36 lipid FF is widely used but its parameters for specific lipid mixtures are not systematically calibrated against experimental data that reports on stoichiometric distributions, such as small-angle scattering or NMR. This gap limits the predictive power of simulations for systems where stoichiometric plasticity is biologically relevant, such as in membrane remodeling or drug delivery vehicles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying Bayesian inference to calibrate lipid FF parameters against experimental observables will yield posterior distributions that, when used in simulation, predict not a single structure but a probabilistic ensemble of coexisting stoichiometries, and that this ensemble will more accurately reflect the inherent dynamic heterogeneity of lipid complexes.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) framework to generate conformational and stoichiometric ensembles for target lipid systems (e.g., DMPC/DHPC bicelles). A Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling protocol, using tools like PyMC3, will be implemented to sample the posterior distribution of key torsional and nonbonded FF parameters. The likelihood function will compare simulation-derived observables (e.g., radius of gyration, scattering profiles) directly to experimental data. The posterior parameter sets will then drive extensive MD simulations to predict the full stoichiometric ensemble and its population distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a statistically rigorous framework for lipid FF calibration that explicitly quantifies parameter uncertainty and its propagation to predicted structural states. It will provide the community with refined CHARMM-compatible parameters for key lipid mixtures and a generalizable method to assess whether experimental data supports a single dominant structure or a dynamic mixture. This moves beyond static, single-structure predictions to a probabilistic understanding of lipid assembly.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods have been applied to biomolecular force fields, their integration with the SILCS platform to directly infer dynamic stoichiometric ensembles from bulk experimental data for lipid complexes is novel. This approach treats multiple interconverting stoichiometries as a core model hypothesis to be tested, rather than a nuisance, which is a significant shift from standard parameterization workflows.

proposals.title: Mapping Lipid Phase Diagrams with SILCS-Guided Metadynamics: A Transfer of Enhanced Sampling Paradigms from Protein-Ligand to Membrane Biophysics
proposals.motivation: Understanding lipid phase behavior (e.g., gel-fluid, liquid-ordered transitions) is critical for modeling membrane protein function, drug-membrane interactions, and the formation of lipid rafts. However, atomistic molecular dynamics simulations of these slow, collective transitions are prohibitively expensive using conventional methods. Enhanced sampling techniques that have revolutionized the study of protein folding and ligand binding have not been systematically adapted and validated for lipid bilayer systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that collective variables (CVs) derived from Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) pharmacophore maps, which capture spatial distributions of chemical functionalities, can serve as effective reaction coordinates for driving and analyzing lipid phase transitions in enhanced sampling simulations.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS-Biologics methodology to generate grand canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC)/MD maps of chemical affinity (e.g., for water, benzene, carbonyl oxygen) across a model lipid bilayer in different phases. These 3D affinity fields will be used to define system-wide CVs for Well-Tempered Metadynamics simulations. The approach will be tested on DPPC and POPC bilayers to study the main phase transition, leveraging the CHARMM36 lipid force field. Key metrics will include convergence of free energy surfaces, transition rates, and comparison to experimental transition enthalpies and temperatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable framework for simulating lipid phase behavior at atomistic resolution, providing quantitative free energy landscapes. It will extend the utility of the SILCS platform from drug discovery to fundamental membrane biophysics, enabling future studies on how cholesterol, peptides, or drugs modulate membrane phase properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing SILCS—a tool designed for mapping protein binding sites for drug design—to identify collective variables for lipid phase transitions, creating a direct methodological bridge between the fields of computational drug discovery and membrane biophysics that did not exist pre-2026.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Inversion for Force Field Optimization: Deriving Sulfonyl Group Parameters from Protein-Ligand Complex Data Prior to Small Molecule Refinement
proposals.motivation: The sulfonyl group is a critical pharmacophore in many drugs, forming key interactions with protein targets. Traditional CHARMM force field parameter optimization typically derives parameters from small molecule quantum mechanical (QM) data and crystal structures first, then validates in biomolecular contexts. This can lead to suboptimal performance in simulating protein-ligand binding, as the condensed-phase, complex environment may modulate electronic properties differently than gas-phase or simple crystalline states. A method to derive parameters directly from the target environment could improve predictive accuracy in structure-based drug design.
proposals.hypothesis: Force field parameters for the sulfonyl moiety, optimized primarily against experimental data from protein-ligand complexes (e.g., crystallographic geometries and binding affinities), will yield more accurate simulations of drug-target interactions than parameters derived solely from hierarchical small molecule data.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform as the primary computational engine. The workflow will: 1) Curate a benchmark set of high-resolution protein-sulfonyl ligand crystal structures and associated binding data. 2) Use SILCS-Biologics for initial protein charge assignment and SILCS-based free energy perturbation (SILCS-FEP) calculations to assess initial parameter performance. 3) Implement an iterative optimization loop where sulfonyl torsion, partial charge, and Lennard-Jones parameters are adjusted to minimize the error in reproducing complex geometries and relative binding free energies, using the SILCS-FEP framework for scoring. 4) Only in a final refinement stage will the optimized parameters be validated and minimally adjusted against small molecule QM and crystal data to ensure transferability.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, target-informed parameter set for sulfonyl groups within the CHARMM force field, directly validated for protein-ligand binding simulations. It will establish a proof-of-concept 'inverted hierarchy' optimization protocol that can be generalized to other critical pharmacophores. The resulting parameters will be disseminated through the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) program and the SILCS platform for immediate use in drug discovery.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard force field development paradigm by prioritizing the complex biological target environment over simple model systems as the primary data source for parameter optimization. While SILCS is used for validation and design, its use as the core engine for *deriving* force field parameters from protein-ligand data is a novel application.

proposals.title: Mapping the Conformational Landscape of Sulfonamide Linkers in Drug-like Molecules Using SILCS-Guided Force Field Parameterization
proposals.motivation: Sulfonamides are critical pharmacophores and backbone linkers in protease inhibitors, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, and constrained peptides, yet their conformational preferences are not systematically characterized in modern biomolecular force fields. Inaccurate torsional parameters for the sulfonamide moiety can lead to poor predictions of binding poses and relative free energies in structure-based drug design, limiting the reliability of computational workflows for sulfonamide-based therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the conformational landscape of sulfonamide groups is more complex and context-dependent than currently modeled in the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF), and that new, high-fidelity torsional parameters derived from quantum mechanical (QM) data and validated via Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) will significantly improve the accuracy of molecular dynamics (MD) simulations for sulfonamide-containing ligands.
proposals.method: We will first perform high-level QM (MP2/cc-pVTZ) scans of model sulfonamide fragments to map torsional energy surfaces. These data will be used to fit new CHARMM-compatible dihedral parameters. The parameters will be validated by performing SILCS simulations, where diverse sulfonamide fragments compete for solvation sites on protein surfaces (e.g., carbonic anhydrase II, thrombin). The SILCS FragMaps will be compared to experimental binding data and crystal structures to assess improvements in predicting sulfonamide-protein interactions and ligand conformational preferences.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, validated set of force field parameters for sulfonamides, directly enhancing the CHARMM/OpenMM ecosystem. It will provide fundamental insights into sulfonamide conformational energetics and establish a robust SILCS-based protocol for validating ligand fragment parameters, thereby improving the accuracy of computational drug design for a major class of compounds.
proposals.novelty: While force field parameterization is routine, this study uniquely integrates high-level QM data with the SILCS-Biologics platform—a method developed in the Mackerell lab—for empirical, protein-environment validation of fragment conformational preferences, moving beyond gas-phase or pure solvent validation.

proposals.title: Validating and Refining Sulfonyl Group Solvation Parameters in the CHARMM General Force Field Using Alchemical Free Energy Calculations
proposals.motivation: The sulfonyl group is a critical pharmacophore in many drugs, including protease inhibitors and sulfonamide antibiotics, where accurate prediction of its binding affinity depends on precise solvation free energies. Current solvation parameters for sulfonyl moieties in the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) are derived from limited experimental data and may not fully capture complex interactions in heterogeneous protein environments. Systematic validation is needed to improve the reliability of computational drug design for sulfonyl-containing ligands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that alchemical free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations, using high-fidelity reference data, will reveal systematic deviations in the solvation thermodynamics of sulfonyl groups predicted by the current CGenFF parameters, enabling targeted refinement.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform to identify diverse sulfonyl-containing fragments and map their solvation sites. Alchemical FEP calculations will be performed using the CHARMM simulation package with the current CGenFF parameters. The relative solvation free energies for a series of sulfonyl-containing small molecules will be computed via thermodynamic integration (TI) and/or multistate Bennett acceptance ratio (MBAR) methods, benchmarking against experimental hydration free energies and high-level quantum mechanical (SMD/MST) solvation data. The workflow will iteratively adjust Lennard-Jones and partial charge parameters to minimize the discrepancy between calculated and reference values.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, refined set of sulfonyl group parameters for CGenFF, directly enhancing the accuracy of binding affinity predictions in structure-based drug design. It will establish a robust FEP-based protocol for force field parameter validation that can be extended to other under-parameterized functional groups.
proposals.novelty: While FEP is commonly used for binding affinity prediction, its direct application to validate and refine the solvation parameters of a specific, pharmaceutically crucial chemical moiety within the established CGenFF framework is novel. This integrates the SILCS fragment mapping approach with rigorous alchemical free energy methods for parameter optimization.

proposals.title: Development of a Sulfonyl Group Coarse-Grained Model via SILCS for Enhanced Binding Site Characterization and Drug Design
proposals.motivation: Sulfonyl groups are critical pharmacophores in many drugs, including sulfonamides and sulfonylureas, mediating key interactions with protein targets. However, atomistic molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of sulfonyl-containing drugs are computationally expensive, limiting the sampling of binding pathways and residence times. A robust coarse-grained (CG) model for the sulfonyl moiety would enable longer timescale simulations to study binding kinetics and thermodynamics, directly addressing a bottleneck in structure-based drug design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transferable coarse-grained model for the sulfonyl group, parameterized using the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, can accurately reproduce its solvation, interaction, and conformational properties, enabling efficient and predictive simulations of drug binding events.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS-Biologics framework to derive CG parameters. First, we will perform all-atom SILCS simulations with sulfonyl-containing probe molecules in aqueous solution to obtain Grid Free Energies (GFEs) for sulfonyl interactions. These GFEs will inform the non-bonded parameters (e.g., effective charges, Lennard-Jones potentials) for a 2- or 3-site CG representation of the sulfonyl group. The bonded parameters (bonds, angles) will be optimized to match conformational distributions from quantum mechanical and atomistic MD reference data. The model will be validated by simulating known sulfonyl drug-protein complexes (e.g., carbonic anhydrase inhibitors) and comparing binding poses and relative affinities to atomistic simulations and experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematically parameterized, transferable CG force field for the sulfonyl chemical moiety, directly integrable with existing CG protein models like the SILCS-Biologics CGenFF. It will enable microsecond-to-millisecond simulations of sulfonyl drug binding, offering new insights into kinetics and cryptic sites. The model will be disseminated as a module for the CHARMM and SILCS software suites, enhancing the community's capability for efficient drug design.
proposals.novelty: While CG models exist for proteins and lipids, a chemistry-specific CG model for the sulfonyl pharmacophore, derived from first-principles SILCS solvation thermodynamics, is novel. This approach moves beyond generic bead representations to a chemically accurate, fragment-based model tailored for a critical drug-design motif.

proposals.title: Systematic Cheminformatics Analysis and Targeted Parameterization of Sulfonyl-Containing Fragments for Improved Force Field Accuracy in SILCS-Based Drug Design
proposals.motivation: The sulfonyl group (R-SO2-R') is a critical pharmacophore in numerous drugs and bioactive molecules, contributing to binding affinity and metabolic stability. However, its accurate representation in molecular mechanics force fields, such as CHARMM, is challenging due to its complex electronic structure and variable chemical environment. Inaccuracies in sulfonyl parameters can propagate errors in SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) simulations, compromising the reliability of fragment-based binding site mapping and ligand design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a large-scale, data-driven identification of the most prevalent sulfonyl fragment chemotypes in drug-like chemical space will reveal specific conformational and electronic profiles, enabling the development of targeted, high-accuracy force field parameters that will significantly improve the predictive power of SILCS for sulfonyl-containing ligands.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale cheminformatics mining of drug databases (e.g., ChEMBL, DrugBank) using the RDKit toolkit to identify, classify, and rank all unique sulfonyl-containing fragment environments. Statistical analysis of torsion angles, bond lengths, and substitution patterns will define parameterization targets. Quantum mechanical calculations (MP2/cc-pVTZ) will provide target data for deriving new CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) parameters for the highest-priority fragments. These parameters will be validated through SILCS simulations of known sulfonyl-drug complexes, comparing binding site maps and relative binding affinities against benchmarks and previous parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, curated library of parameterized sulfonyl fragments directly usable in SILCS and molecular dynamics workflows. It will establish a generalizable fragment-analysis framework for prioritizing force field development efforts based on pharmaceutical relevance. The improved parameters will enhance the accuracy of SILCS for designing and optimizing sulfonyl-based inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While sulfonyl parameters exist, this approach is novel in its direct linkage of large-scale cheminformatics prevalence analysis to targeted force field development, specifically for the SILCS platform. It moves beyond generic parameterization to a data-informed strategy focused on the fragments most impactful for drug discovery.

proposals.title: Validating and Refining SILCS-Based Ligand Binding Poses Using Experimental NMR Order Parameters for Sulfonyl Group Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of ligand binding modes, particularly for flexible functional groups like sulfonamides, is critical for structure-based drug design but remains challenging. The Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) method provides detailed maps of functional group binding affinities, but its predictions of group dynamics and conformational preferences lack direct experimental validation. NMR-derived order parameters (S²) offer a powerful, quantitative measure of bond-vector dynamics that can directly test these computational predictions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating experimental NMR order parameters as validation targets will reveal systematic biases in SILCS simulations of sulfonyl group dynamics and that using these parameters as restraints during SILCS Monte Carlo sampling will yield more accurate and experimentally consistent ligand binding poses.
proposals.method: We will select a set of pharmaceutically relevant protein-sulfonamide ligand complexes with available backbone and sidechain NMR order parameters. SILCS simulations will be performed using the CHARMM36 force field with the CGenFF parameters for sulfonamides. The conformational sampling and dynamics of the sulfonyl moiety in the SILCS FragMaps will be directly compared to experimental S² values. Subsequently, a modified SILCS-Biologics protocol will be developed where the NMR order parameters are incorporated as harmonic restraints on the relevant torsion angles during the ligand pose sampling and scoring stage, biasing the ensemble toward experimentally observed dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for integrating NMR dynamics data into the SILCS platform, moving beyond static structural validation. It will provide a critical assessment of force field performance for sulfonyl groups and deliver a refined, experimentally guided protocol for predicting the binding modes of this ubiquitous pharmacophore, enhancing the reliability of SILCS for covalent and non-covalent drug design.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been validated against crystallographic data and binding affinities, its direct integration with NMR dynamics data for specific functional group validation is unprecedented. The development of a dynamics-restrained SILCS sampling approach represents a novel synthesis of computational mapping and experimental biophysical measurement.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Virtual Screening of Sulfonyl-Containing Compound Libraries Using SILCS-Based Force Fields and Ensemble Docking
proposals.motivation: Sulfonyl groups are privileged pharmacophores in drug discovery, present in protease inhibitors, kinase inhibitors, and anti-diabetic agents. However, accurate virtual screening of sulfonyl-containing libraries is hampered by the need for precise modeling of their complex electrostatic and conformational properties. A scalable, physics-based approach would enable the efficient identification of novel sulfonyl-based leads for challenging targets.
proposals.hypothesis: That the integration of the recently developed, high-accuracy CHARMM Drude polarizable force field for sulfonamides with the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform will enable high-throughput, structure-based virtual screening with improved predictive power for sulfonyl compound binding affinities and poses.
proposals.method: We will first parameterize a diverse library of sulfonyl-containing compounds using the Drude-2019 force field. The SILCS methodology will be employed to generate target-specific functional site maps (FragMaps) for competitive binding hotspots. High-throughput docking will be performed using SILCS-based pharmacophore constraints and ensemble receptor conformations from SILCS-MD simulations. Top-ranking hits will be refined with SILCS-guided free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations to predict binding affinities.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, scalable pipeline for the virtual screening of sulfonyl-based chemical space, directly integrating polarizable force fields with the SILCS platform. It will deliver a validated, publicly available library of sulfonyl compounds with SILCS-FragMaps and benchmarked binding predictions, providing a new resource for the drug discovery community.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been used for fragment and covalent drug design, its integration with a polarizable force field for systematic, high-throughput screening of a specific, pharmaceutically important chemotype (sulfonyl) represents a novel and targeted advancement beyond standard docking methods.

proposals.title: Integration of Machine-Learned Quantum Mechanical Potentials into SILCS for Ab Initio Accuracy in Protein-Ligand Binding Free Energy Calculations
proposals.motivation: Classical molecular mechanics (MM) force fields, like CHARMM, are foundational for structure-based drug design but have known limitations in describing electronic polarization, charge transfer, and complex non-covalent interactions critical for accurate ligand binding predictions. Machine-learned interatomic potentials (MLIPs) trained on quantum chemical data, such as those from the ANI or PhysNet architectures, promise near-quantum mechanical accuracy at MM computational cost, yet their robust integration into workflows for pharmaceutical discovery remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That embedding a MLIP, specifically fine-tuned for biomolecular fragments, within the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) framework will yield binding free energy predictions and functional group maps with significantly improved accuracy over classical force fields, while maintaining the high-throughput screening capability essential for drug design.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid ML/MM SILCS protocol. A graph neural network potential (e.g., SchNet or a modified ANI-2x) will be trained on a bespoke dataset of QM (DFT) calculations for protein-ligand fragment interactions in diverse chemical environments. This MLIP will replace the classical MM potential during the SILCS Monte Carlo sampling of functional group distributions. The MLIP's energy and force evaluations will be integrated via a client-server architecture to manage computational load. Binding affinities for a test set of pharmaceutically relevant protein-ligand complexes will be computed and validated against experimental data and rigorous alchemical free energy calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available SILCS-MLIP simulation platform that delivers ab initio accuracy in biomolecular recognition. It will provide a critical benchmark for the practical value of MLIPs in drug discovery, potentially setting a new standard for the precision of computational fragment-based design and binding site identification.
proposals.novelty: While MLIPs have been applied to small molecules and proteins in isolation, their direct integration into the SILCS methodology—a high-throughput, explicit-solvent, fragment-sampling platform—to generate quantum-accurate functional group maps for drug design is a novel and transformative application beyond current proof-of-concept studies.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of CHARMM-Compatible Force Field Parameters for Common and Combinatorial Protein Post-Translational Modifications
proposals.motivation: Post-translational modifications (PTMs) are critical regulators of protein structure, dynamics, and function, but their accurate simulation is hindered by a lack of comprehensive, high-quality force field parameters. Pre-2026, most parameters are available only for isolated, common PTMs, neglecting the combinatorial complexity and altered physicochemical properties seen in biological systems. This gap limits the reliability of molecular dynamics (MD) simulations for studying PTM-driven processes like signaling, allostery, and drug binding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic, physics-based parameterization of common and co-occurring PTMs, integrated into the CHARMM force field, will significantly improve the accuracy of MD simulations in capturing the structural and energetic effects of PTMs on proteins and their interactions with ligands.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology as a core tool. For each target PTM (e.g., phosphorylation, acetylation, methylation) and key combinations, we will: 1) Derive bonded and partial charge parameters using quantum mechanical (QM) calculations at the MP2/cc-pVTZ//HF/6-31G(d) level, following the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) protocol. 2) Use SILCS-Biologics to map interaction fields and validate partial charges by comparing calculated and experimental hydration free energies. 3) Validate torsional parameters against QM rotational profiles and final parameters against crystal structures and NMR data of PTM-modified peptides/proteins.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available, rigorously validated parameter database for ~15 major PTMs and their pairwise combinations, directly usable with CHARMM and compatible simulation packages. It will enable more realistic simulations of PTM-enriched systems, advancing structure-based drug design against modified targets and the mechanistic study of epigenetic regulation and signaling networks.
proposals.novelty: While PTM parameters exist, this project uniquely focuses on the systematic parameterization of *combinatorial* PTM states, whose energetics are non-additive, and leverages the SILCS platform for integrated charge validation and binding site analysis, a novel application of Dr. MacKerell's established tools.

proposals.title: Optimizing the CHARMM Force Field for Ligand Binding Kinetics via Integration of SILCS and Markov State Model Data
proposals.motivation: Classical force fields like CHARMM are primarily parameterized to reproduce equilibrium structures and thermodynamics, yet drug efficacy often depends on binding/unbinding kinetics (k_on, k_off). Pre-2026, methods like SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) excel at mapping binding sites and affinities, but a systematic framework to tune force field parameters specifically for kinetic accuracy is lacking. This limits the predictive power of molecular dynamics (MD) for optimizing drug residence times.
proposals.hypothesis: That re-parameterizing torsional and non-bonded parameters in the CHARMM force field against experimental and computationally derived kinetic transition rates—rather than solely structural data—will yield a force field variant (CHARMM-Kin) that more accurately predicts ligand binding and unbinding pathways and rates.
proposals.method: We will leverage the SILCS-Biologics platform to generate conformational ensembles and identify key ligand-protein interaction hotspots. Target kinetic data will be obtained from published experimental residence times and from Markov State Models (MSMs) built from ultra-long MD simulations of model systems (e.g., trypsin inhibitors, kinase ligands). A gradient-based optimization algorithm will iteratively adjust specific force field dihedral and Lennard-Jones parameters to minimize the difference between simulated (from MSMs) and target state transition rates, while maintaining fidelity to equilibrium SILCS free energy maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first proof-of-concept, kinetics-optimized force field parameter set within the CHARMM framework. It will provide a novel computational workflow that integrates SILCS, MSMs, and force field optimization for kinetic drug design. The resulting tool will directly support the design of ligands with tailored binding/unbinding rates, moving beyond static affinity predictions.
proposals.novelty: While force field optimization is common, targeting kinetic observables as primary fitting data is a paradigm shift from the standard focus on equilibrium structures and thermodynamics. The direct integration of SILCS spatial mapping with kinetic parameter optimization is a novel synergy of Dr. Mackerell's established methodologies for a new goal.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation and Refinement of CHARMM Dipeptide Backbone Torsional Parameters for Improved Transferability to Protein Environments
proposals.motivation: The CHARMM additive force field derives key backbone dihedral parameters from quantum mechanical calculations on small dipeptide analogues, assuming their full transferability to folded proteins. This assumption is foundational to molecular mechanics but may be an oversimplification, as the local electrostatic and steric environment within a protein can differ significantly from an isolated dipeptide. Subtle errors in backbone conformational preferences could propagate, affecting the accuracy of protein-ligand binding predictions, protein design, and the identification of functional sites using methods like SILCS.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the torsional energy profiles for protein backbone dihedrals (Φ/Ψ) are context-dependent and that refining dipeptide-derived parameters against experimental conformational distributions in folded proteins will improve the force field's accuracy for modeling protein structure, dynamics, and interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology as a core computational framework. First, we will perform extensive molecular dynamics simulations of a diverse set of globular proteins using the current CHARMM36m force field. We will then use SILCS-Biologics to map backbone conformational preferences and compare them to high-resolution crystallographic data. Systematic deviations will be identified, and targeted quantum mechanical calculations on model systems mimicking the protein environment will be performed. New torsional parameters will be derived and iteratively tested via free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations within the SILCS platform to assess improvements in reproducing experimental backbone propensities and ligand-binding site geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, data-driven assessment of a core assumption in biomolecular force field development. It will yield a refined set of backbone parameters with demonstrably improved transferability, directly enhancing the accuracy of the CHARMM force field for protein structure prediction and drug design applications. The integrated SILCS-based workflow will also serve as a generalizable protocol for future force field validation and refinement.
proposals.novelty: While force field validation is common, this proposal uniquely leverages the SILCS platform—specifically its ability to map conformational landscapes and compute free energies—to systematically diagnose and correct for environmental dependence in foundational dipeptide parameters, moving beyond simple validation to targeted, physics-based refinement.

proposals.title: Systematic Force Field Parameter Optimization via Crystal Simulation Ensemble Analysis (CrySEA)
proposals.motivation: Molecular mechanics force fields are traditionally parameterized using quantum mechanical data and small molecule properties, with crystal lattice simulations serving primarily as a final validation step. This validation-centric approach underutilizes the rich, high-resolution structural and thermodynamic data encoded in experimentally determined crystal structures. A method that systematically inverts this paradigm, using crystal simulations as a primary data source for optimization, could lead to more accurate and transferable parameters for drug-like molecules and their interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: That iterative optimization of CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) parameters against a diverse ensemble of crystal simulation observables—including lattice energies, unit cell dimensions, and molecular conformations—will yield parameters with superior predictive power for ligand binding and solvation compared to those derived from standard protocols.
proposals.method: We will develop the Crystal Simulation Ensemble Analysis (CrySEA) workflow. The core method integrates the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform to simulate small-molecule crystals in their full periodic boundary conditions. An automated pipeline will run molecular dynamics simulations for a curated library of 50-100 pharmaceutically relevant molecules with known crystal structures. A bespoke objective function will quantify the agreement between simulated and experimental lattice parameters, energies (using the LAMMPS interface), and torsional distributions. Optimization will be performed using a hybrid approach, combining gradient-free algorithms (e.g., simplex) for dihedral parameters with Bayesian optimization for Lennard-Jones and partial atomic charge refinements, all within the established CGenFF topology framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available parameter optimization workflow that directly targets solid-state observables, a critical but underused data dimension. It will produce a refined set of CGenFF parameters for common drug scaffolds, demonstrably improving the accuracy of binding affinity predictions in SILCS-based virtual screening and free energy perturbation calculations. The methodology will establish crystal simulations as a foundational tool for force field development, not just validation.
proposals.novelty: While crystal simulations are routinely used for validation, their systematic application as the primary driver for automated, multi-property force field parameter optimization is unprecedented. This inverts the traditional development pipeline, directly targeting the condensed-phase environmental forces crucial for biomolecular recognition.

proposals.title: Quantifying Hydrophobic Recognition: A SILCS-Driven Computational and Experimental Study of Shape Complementarity in Apolar Solvents
proposals.motivation: The hydrophobic effect is a cornerstone of biomolecular recognition and drug binding, but its contribution is often entangled with other interactions like hydrogen bonding. A rigorous, isolated quantification of how shape complementarity and packing efficiency alone drive selective binding is lacking. This knowledge gap limits our fundamental understanding and our ability to design ligands for purely hydrophobic binding sites, which are common in protein targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a non-polar, non-competitive environment, the binding affinity and selectivity between a rigid synthetic host and purely aliphatic guests will be dictated primarily by the complementarity of their hydrophobic surfaces and the efficiency of van der Waals packing, independent of electrostatic or polar interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, leveraging its proven ability to map functional group binding affinities. SILCS simulations will be performed on a model host-guest system (e.g., a glycoluril-based molecular clip or cavitand) in an explicit apolar solvent (e.g., cyclohexane). The SILCS-Biologics framework will be used to compute binding free energies for a congeneric series of branched and linear alkane guests. These computational predictions will be validated by synthesizing the host and measuring binding affinities via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) in the same apolar solvent.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative dataset on hydrophobic recognition decoupled from other forces, directly testing the 'lock-and-key' model for non-polar interactions. It will validate and extend the SILCS platform for applications in extreme hydrophobic environments, informing the design of inhibitors for protein pockets dominated by aliphatic or aromatic residues.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been used for proteins in aqueous solution, applying it to a minimalist synthetic system in a purely apolar solvent to isolate hydrophobic forces is a novel and adversarial test of the method's physical foundations and of the hydrophobic effect itself.

proposals.title: A SILCS-Melt Framework for Predicting Metal-Ligand Complexation in Silicate Melts Using Environment-Specific Force Field Parameterization
proposals.motivation: Predicting metal speciation in high-temperature silicate melts is critical for geochemistry and materials science, but current models rely heavily on analogies to aqueous solution thermodynamics, which fail to capture the unique, crowded, and highly polarizable environment of melts. This creates significant uncertainty in models for magmatic processes and glass design. A new paradigm, inspired by the shift in protein folding from dilute solution to crowded cellular environment models, is needed to treat melts as a distinct solvent with emergent chemical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Metal-ligand complexation equilibria and mechanisms in silicate melts can be accurately predicted by developing a computational framework that uses melt-specific descriptors (e.g., optical basicity, network polymerization) to parameterize environment-aware force fields, explicitly moving beyond aqueous-phase analogies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, which uses molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with explicit solvent and small molecule probes to map functional group binding affinities. Instead of water, the solvent will be a model silicate melt (e.g., Na2O-SiO2). Key melt properties (optical basicity, cation field strength) will be used to guide the parameterization of the non-bonded terms for the melt matrix and metal cations within the CHARMM Drude polarizable force field, for which Mackerell's group has extensive expertise. SILCS simulations will then be performed with ligand fragments to compute melt-specific binding free energies and identify preferred coordination sites for metals, establishing a database of melt-phase stability trends.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computational framework for *ab initio* prediction of metal complexation in melts, generating melt-specific 'pharmacophore' maps analogous to protein binding sites. It will provide a foundational set of validated, melt-parameterized force field terms and establish a new paradigm for modeling speciation that is decoupled from aqueous chemistry, directly impacting predictive models in igneous petrology and metallurgical slag chemistry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical translation of a biological solvation modeling approach (SILCS) to an inorganic, high-temperature condensed phase, and in systematically parameterizing force fields based on intrinsic melt properties rather than extrapolating from aqueous data.

proposals.title: Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification of CHARMM Lipid Force Field Transferability Across Aqueous and Molten Phases
proposals.motivation: Molecular dynamics simulations of lipids and self-assembling systems are crucial for drug delivery and membrane protein studies. A critical, often unverified, assumption is that force field parameters optimized for aqueous bilayers remain valid for non-aqueous phases like dehydrated lipid films or molten states encountered in formulation. Pre-2026, parameter transferability is typically assessed via point estimates, lacking a rigorous, probabilistic framework to quantify uncertainty and identify systematically biased interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Bayesian inference framework will reveal significant, quantifiable posterior uncertainty and systematic bias in key lipid force field parameters (e.g., tail torsion, headgroup partial charges, ion binding Lennard-Jones terms) when transferred between aqueous and molten phases, challenging the assumption of direct transferability.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology to generate target data (e.g., fragment binding free energy maps, density profiles) for a model lipid (e.g., DPPC) in both aqueous bilayer and molten phases. Using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling within a Bayesian framework, we will infer posterior distributions for selected CHARMM lipid force field parameters. The analysis will focus on the Kullback-Leibler divergence between posteriors from different phases and the predictive error when using one phase's posterior to simulate the other.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, uncertainty-aware protocol for assessing force field transferability, moving beyond qualitative comparisons. It will produce a probabilistic 'diagnostic map' identifying which specific inter- and intra-molecular terms are most phase-sensitive, providing direct guidance for future force field development for multi-phase systems in drug design.
proposals.novelty: While Bayesian methods have been applied to force field optimization, their use for rigorously quantifying the uncertainty and bias in parameter transferability between distinct physical phases is novel. This approach integrates the SILCS-Biologics framework for target data with probabilistic inference to solve a fundamental problem in molecular simulation parameterization.

proposals.title: Stoichiometric Phase Diagrams for Protein Complexes: A SILCS-Biologics and Experimental Framework for Mapping Assembly Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Current structure-based drug and biologics design often assumes a single, dominant stoichiometry for target protein complexes, which is an oversimplification. This can lead to inaccurate predictions of binding affinities and off-target effects, as the functional oligomeric state may shift with cellular conditions. A quantitative, condition-dependent map of complex stoichiometry is therefore critical for robust computational design.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability of specific oligomeric states in protein complexes is governed by definable phase boundaries as a function of subunit concentration and solution conditions, analogous to compositional phases in materials science. These boundaries can be predicted computationally and validated experimentally.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS-Biologics platform for structure-based protein charge prediction under varied buffer conditions to compute intermolecular interaction free energies. These will inform coarse-grained Monte Carlo simulations to predict populations of different oligomeric states. Predictions will be tested experimentally using analytical ultracentrifugation and native mass spectrometry on model systems (e.g., tumor necrosis factor family trimers/dimers), systematically varying subunit concentration, pH, and ionic strength to empirically map stability regions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational-experimental framework to predict and validate the condition-dependent stoichiometry of protein complexes. It will directly enhance the accuracy of the SILCS platform for drug and biologics design by accounting for oligomeric state heterogeneity. The resulting 'stoichiometric phase diagrams' will offer a new paradigm for understanding allosteric regulation and complex assembly.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of the phase diagram concept from materials science to map the stability of biomolecular complexes is a novel conceptual leap. Integrating the SILCS-Biologics computational suite with high-resolution biophysical experiments to construct these diagrams represents a unique methodological synthesis.

proposals.title: A Multi-Probe Experimental Suite for the Validation of Computationally Predicted Protein-Ligand Complex Stability
proposals.motivation: Computational methods like Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) and free energy perturbation (FEP) are central to modern drug design, but their predictions of binding stability and complex structure require rigorous experimental validation. Relying on a single experimental technique can be misleading due to inherent limitations and artifacts. A multi-faceted validation strategy, analogous to the convergent evidence approach used in structural biology, is needed to build confidence in computational predictions and guide method refinement.
proposals.hypothesis: That a complementary suite of biophysical experiments—measuring thermodynamics, solution-state assembly, and binding kinetics—will provide a robust, convergent validation of computationally predicted protein-ligand complex stability and stoichiometry, revealing discrepancies invisible to single-technique approaches.
proposals.method: We will apply the SILCS platform to identify and design ligands for a target protein, generating stability predictions for multiple binding modes and stoichiometries. These predictions will be tested using an integrated experimental suite: Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) for binding affinity and thermodynamics, Analytical Ultracentrifugation (AUC) for complex stoichiometry and oligomeric state in solution, and time-resolved fluorescence anisotropy for binding kinetics and conformational dynamics. Data from all techniques will be analyzed in concert to validate or challenge the computational models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a standardized, multi-probe framework for the rigorous validation of computational drug design predictions, moving beyond single-metric validation. It will provide a critical benchmark for SILCS and related methods, identifying specific strengths and failure modes in stability prediction. The resulting datasets will be invaluable for improving force fields and scoring functions.
proposals.novelty: While multi-technique validation is standard in structural biology, its systematic application as a dedicated benchmark for computational stability predictions in drug design is not routine. The explicit integration of AUC for solution-state validation and kinetic probes alongside thermodynamic measurements creates a uniquely comprehensive check on *in silico* models.

proposals.title: Experimental Validation of Inverted Force Fields for Protein-Ligand Complex Stability Using SILCS-Guided Inverse Scattering
proposals.motivation: Accurate force fields are critical for structure-based drug design, but their parameterization for complex, multi-component systems remains challenging and often relies on fitting to limited data. Pre-2026, the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) framework has proven powerful for mapping binding sites and predicting binding affinities, yet its integration with direct experimental derivation of force field parameters is underexplored. This gap limits the predictive power of computational models for complex stability across diverse stoichiometries and solution conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: That force field parameters for molecular complexes, directly inverted from X-ray and neutron scattering data using a SILCS-informed framework, will yield accurate, transferable predictions of stability and structure when validated against orthogonal biophysical measurements.
proposals.method: We will employ an inverse scattering approach, using explicit-solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations within the SILCS platform to generate conformational ensembles for target protein-ligand systems.
