These ensembles will guide the inversion of small-angle X-ray and neutron scattering (SAXS/SANS) data to derive effective intermolecular force field parameters. The inverted force fields will then be used to predict complex stability (e.g., binding free energies via SILCS-based free energy perturbation) and oligomeric states. These predictions will be rigorously tested using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), analytical ultracentrifugation (AUC), and spectroscopic techniques, creating a closed feedback loop to iteratively refine the inversion algorithm.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, experimentally grounded workflow for force field parameterization specifically tailored to complex biomolecular interactions. It will directly demonstrate the utility of integrating SILCS with inverse scattering for predictive drug design. The resulting validated, system-specific force fields will enhance the accuracy of stability predictions for challenging targets, including those with cryptic or allosteric sites.
proposals.novelty: The integration of the SILCS methodology, a proven tool for binding site identification, with an inverse scattering approach for direct force field parameterization is a novel synthesis. Furthermore, the systematic use of a multi-technique experimental suite (scattering, calorimetry, AUC) for both inversion input and validation creates a uniquely rigorous, self-correcting parameterization cycle not previously implemented.

proposals.title: Elucidating Host-Guest Complexation Pathways and Kinetics via SILCS-Guided Enhanced Sampling Molecular Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Understanding the detailed pathways and kinetics of ligand binding to proteins or inclusion within supramolecular hosts is critical for rational drug and materials design. Current methods like Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) excel at mapping binding free energies but provide limited dynamical insight into the multi-step processes, such as desolvation and conformational selection, that govern complexation rates and mechanisms. Integrating SILCS with advanced sampling techniques can bridge this gap, offering a predictive framework for kinetics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying enhanced sampling methods, specifically temperature-accelerated molecular dynamics (TAMD) and parallel tempering metadynamics, to SILCS-derived collective variables will enable the accurate reconstruction of complexation pathways and the calculation of kinetic rates for diverse host-guest systems.
proposals.method: We will employ the SILCS-Biologics platform to generate initial conformational ensembles and identify key interaction pharmacophores (FragMaps) for model systems (e.g., cyclodextrin-guest complexes). These FragMaps will inform the selection of collective variables (CVs) such as host-guest distance and guest orientation. Enhanced sampling will be performed using GROMACS/PLUMED, implementing TAMD to efficiently explore the CV space and metadynamics to reconstruct the free energy landscape. The CHARMM36 force field and CGenFF will be used for all simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated computational workflow that quantitatively predicts not only binding affinities but also the mechanistic steps and timescales of complexation. It will provide foundational insights into kinetic selectivity in molecular recognition, directly impacting the design of drugs with optimized residence times and functional materials with controlled release properties.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS is established for binding site identification and free energy calculations, its integration with advanced sampling MD methods from protein folding to explicitly model and quantify the kinetic pathways of host-guest complexation is a novel and non-trivial extension of the methodology.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware, Multi-Fidelity Machine Learning for Force Field Correction in Host-Guest Binding Free Energy Calculations
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of host-guest binding affinities is critical for drug design but is limited by the accuracy of molecular mechanics force fields. Traditional parametrization against high-level quantum mechanical (QM) data can be biased by the choice of reference method and training set, leading to poor transferability. A robust, self-correcting framework is needed to systematically improve force field accuracy for non-covalent interactions while quantifying predictive uncertainty.
proposals.hypothesis: A machine learning potential trained on a multi-fidelity QM ensemble, weighted by agreement with high-accuracy benchmarks, and iteratively refined via active learning driven by uncertainty quantification, will yield more accurate and transferable corrections for host-guest binding free energy predictions than static, single-fidelity training.
proposals.method: We will integrate the SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform for conformational sampling of host-guest pairs (e.g., cyclodextrins, cucurbiturils) with a multi-fidelity Gaussian Process (GP) or Deep Neural Network model. The model will be trained on energies and forces from an ensemble of QM methods (e.g., DFT-D3, MP2, CCSD(T) on select dimers), with fidelities weighted by their deviation from a small gold-standard set (e.g., DLPNO-CCSD(T)/CBS or experimental ΔG). An integrated uncertainty engine (e.g., using GP variance or ensemble model disagreement) will identify poorly sampled, high-uncertainty conformations during SILCS simulations, triggering on-the-fly high-level QM calculations for those points. This creates an iterative loop of simulation, uncertainty assessment, targeted QM computation, and model retraining.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, uncertainty-quantified force field correction module specifically optimized for host-guest binding, directly integrable with the SILCS methodology. It will provide a generalizable protocol for creating self-improving, chemically accurate potentials with known confidence limits, advancing the reliability of computational drug design and protein-ligand binding studies.
proposals.novelty: The integration of multi-fidelity learning with active, uncertainty-driven sampling for force field refinement moves beyond static QM training sets. This creates a closed-loop, adaptive system that directly addresses model bias and knowledge gaps, a significant advance over single-step parametrization approaches common pre-2026.

proposals.title: Adaptive Force Field Refinement for Host-Guest Systems via On-the-Fly Error Sensing and Machine Learning Correction
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding affinities is critical for structure-based drug design, but classical force fields often lack the precision needed for reliable free energy calculations due to approximations in their functional forms and parameters. Systematically improving force fields for specific, critical interactions remains a labor-intensive process requiring extensive quantum mechanical (QM) benchmarking and manual parameterization, which does not adapt to novel chemical environments encountered in simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive simulation framework, which uses real-time error estimators to trigger targeted high-fidelity machine learning (ML) potential calculations, can both correct simulation trajectories on-the-fly and autonomously generate focused datasets to iteratively refine classical force field parameters for problematic host-guest interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework integrating the CHARMM general force field (CGenFF) with a high-accuracy ML potential (e.g., a graph neural network or Gaussian Approximation Potential). An error sensor, based on local geometric descriptors (e.g., symmetry-adapted functions) and electronic structure indicators (e.g., from semiempirical methods), will flag configurations where host-guest interactions are likely poorly described. For these configurations, the ML potential, pre-trained on QM data for relevant fragments, will compute energies/forces. These ML-derived data will feed an active learning loop to retrain the ML model and, crucially, will be used in a SILCS-like competitive saturation protocol to optimize torsional and nonbonded parameters in CGenFF for the identified interaction motifs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, automated methodology for force field refinement targeted at specific chemical interactions, moving beyond uniform parameter fitting. It will provide a proof-of-concept adaptive simulation engine that balances computational efficiency with high accuracy, directly applicable to improving binding affinity predictions in drug discovery projects. The resulting self-improving force field paradigm reduces reliance on exhaustive QM scans.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual analogy to CFD hybrid modeling (RANS-LES) applied to molecular simulation, creating a closed-loop system where error detection drives both instantaneous correction and long-term force field parameter optimization. This moves beyond static ML/MM schemes by using the ML potential as a diagnostic and refinement tool for the classical model itself.

proposals.title: Integrating Paramagnetic NMR, Photo-CIDNP, and SILCS-MD to Map Transient States and Free Energy Landscapes in Protein-Ligand Binding
proposals.motivation: Conventional structural biology and ensemble-averaged NMR often fail to capture sparsely populated, transient conformational states that are critical for understanding binding mechanisms, allostery, and drug efficacy. A comprehensive, experimentally restrained map of the dynamic energy landscape governing host-guest interactions remains a major unmet challenge in rational drug design and molecular biophysics.
proposals.hypothesis: The synergistic application of paramagnetic relaxation enhancement (PRE) NMR and photo-chemically induced dynamic nuclear polarization (photo-CIDNP) NMR can provide explicit, long-range distance and solvent accessibility restraints for low-population states, which, when integrated into the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) molecular dynamics framework, will yield a high-resolution model of the complete binding free energy landscape.
proposals.method: We will employ PRE NMR using site-specifically attached paramagnetic tags (e.g., MTSL) and photo-CIDNP with flavin photosensitizers to probe transient states in model host-guest systems (e.g., cyclophilin-ligand complexes). These experimental observables will be converted into explicit spatial restraints for SILCS-based molecular dynamics simulations using the CHARMM force field. The SILCS-MD protocol will be extended to incorporate these time-resolved restraints, guiding the sampling of rare states and enabling the direct calculation of free energy surfaces. Key predictions of transition pathways and energetics will be validated using single-molecule force spectroscopy (optical tweezers).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, generalizable pipeline for experimentally deriving energy landscapes of biomolecular interactions, moving beyond static structures. It will provide unprecedented insight into the role of excited states in molecular recognition. The resulting methodology will directly enhance structure-based drug design by enabling the targeting of cryptic or allosteric sites accessed via these transient states.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS and advanced NMR methods exist separately, their integration to use paramagnetic and photo-CIDNP data as dynamic restraints for free energy landscape calculation is unprecedented. This creates a closed loop where specialized NMR populates and detects rare states, SILCS-MD models the full landscape, and single-molecule experiments provide orthogonal validation.

proposals.title: Quantifying Supramolecular Exchange Dynamics via NMR Relaxation Dispersion: A SILCS-Informed Approach to Host-Guest Energy Landscapes
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular host-guest interactions are central to drug delivery, sensing, and catalysis, yet our understanding is largely static, derived from crystal structures or average solution measurements. Critical dynamic processes—guest exchange, translocation, and host adaptation—occur on micro-to-millisecond timescales and remain experimentally inaccessible, creating a major gap between structural models and functional behavior. Pre-2026, the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) method provides detailed thermodynamic maps of binding sites but lacks direct experimental validation of the kinetics governing these complex landscapes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solution-state NMR relaxation dispersion (RD) experiments, specifically CPMG and R1ρ, can be robustly applied to supramolecular systems to detect and quantify the kinetics, thermodynamics, and structural features of 'invisible' excited states involved in guest exchange and host dynamics, providing a direct experimental benchmark for SILCS-computed free energy landscapes.
proposals.method: We will apply 13C/1H CPMG and off-resonance R1ρ relaxation dispersion experiments to model host-guest systems (e.g., cyclodextrins, cucurbiturils with drug-like guests). Site-specific RD profiles will be fit to two- or three-state exchange models using established software (e.g., CATIA, ChemEx) to extract exchange rates (kex), populations (pB), and chemical shift differences (Δω) of transient states. SILCS simulations, including Grand Canonical Monte Carlo (GCMC) and Molecular Dynamics (MD) with the CHARMM36 force field, will be performed to generate 3D free energy fields (FragMaps) and identify low-population binding modes, whose predicted chemical shifts and energetics will be directly compared to RD-derived parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental NMR framework for quantifying the kinetic and thermodynamic parameters of supramolecular dynamic processes, moving the field beyond static snapshots. It will provide the first direct experimental validation and refinement of SILCS-predicted excited-state binding modes and energy barriers, strengthening the method's utility for dynamic drug design. The resulting datasets will serve as crucial benchmarks for next-generation force field parameterization in the CHARMM family.
proposals.novelty: While NMR RD is a gold standard in protein dynamics, its systematic application to synthetic supramolecular systems to map exchange landscapes is unprecedented. The tight integration of these quantitative experiments with SILCS computational mapping creates a unique, closed-loop workflow for elucidating the full energy landscape of host-guest complexation.

proposals.title: NMR-Constrained Coarse-Grained Force Field Development for Mechanistic Simulations of Endoplasmic Reticulum Membrane Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Organelle membranes, like the endoplasmic reticulum (ER), are compositionally complex and dynamic, hosting critical processes in protein folding, lipid synthesis, and drug metabolism. Current molecular simulations lack the scale to model these large, heterogeneous membranes while also accurately capturing the specific lipid-protein and lipid-drug interactions that govern their function. A multi-scale approach, directly validated by experimental dynamics, is needed to bridge this gap and enable predictive modeling of organelle-scale membrane biology and pharmacology.
proposals.hypothesis: That a coarse-grained (CG) force field, whose parameters are directly optimized against solution-state NMR data on lipid dynamics and interactions within membrane mimetics, will yield large-scale simulations of organelle membranes that retain atomic-scale accuracy for molecular recognition events.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform as the foundational framework. Solution-state NMR data (e.g., NOEs, relaxation, PREs) on lipid headgroup and acyl chain dynamics, as well as on host-guest complexes (e.g., lipid with a sterol or a drug-like molecule) in bicelles or nanodiscs, will be acquired. These experimental constraints will be used to iteratively parameterize and validate a new CG force field within the SILCS-Biologics/CG infrastructure. The final validated model will be used to run microsecond-scale CG molecular dynamics simulations of large (≥ 50 nm x 50 nm), compositionally accurate ER membrane patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, NMR-validated CG force field specifically tuned for organelle membranes, enabling unprecedented simulations of their large-scale structure and dynamics. It will provide a novel computational platform to visualize and quantify the molecular mechanisms of membrane remodeling, protein insertion, and small molecule partitioning in a physiologically relevant context, directly impacting drug design and membrane biology.
proposals.novelty: The direct, iterative use of solution-state NMR dynamics as quantitative constraints for CG force field parameterization, moving beyond static structural fitting, to ensure the model captures the essential motional timescales and interaction energetics critical for function in large-scale organelle membrane simulations.

proposals.title: Inverse Stability Design of Host-Guest Complexes Using SILCS-Guided Fragment Assembly and Free Energy Optimization
proposals.motivation: Rational drug and material design often requires fine-tuning binding affinity, but current methods typically start with a ligand and optimize its properties. A true inverse design, starting from a target binding free energy and a host structure to generate novel guest molecules, remains a significant challenge. This capability would enable the precise engineering of binders for applications ranging from therapeutic agents with tailored pharmacokinetics to selective sensors and supramolecular assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting principles from computational protein sequence design and integrating them with the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform, it is possible to computationally assemble guest molecules from fragment libraries that are predicted to bind a given host with a pre-specified, user-defined binding free energy.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework that integrates the SILCS methodology to define fragment binding pharmacophores (FragMaps) for a target host protein or macrocycle. An inverse design algorithm, analogous to RosettaDesign, will then perform a Monte Carlo search over a library of synthetically feasible fragments, assembling them into complete guest molecules. Each candidate will be scored using a hybrid function combining SILCS-computed Gibbs free energy contributions (ΔG) from FragMaps, a CHARMM-based molecular mechanics/generalized Born surface area (MM/GBSA) refinement term, and a machine-learned synthetic accessibility score trained on pre-2026 reaction databases. The search will be constrained to optimize for a target ΔG value.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational paradigm for 'dialing-in' binding affinity, moving beyond simple ranking to generative design for a specific stability target. It will extend the SILCS platform into a proactive design tool for novel chemical matter. The resulting open-source framework will provide a new approach for designing tailored inhibitors, chemical probes, and supramolecular guests.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS is used for site identification and covalent drug design, its application as the scoring core for an inverse stability design algorithm is unprecedented. This inverts the typical design workflow by treating the desired binding free energy as a direct input, not an output, for molecule generation.

proposals.title: Modulating Drug Binding Kinetics via a Supramolecular Relay: Computational and Experimental Characterization of Cyclodextrin Guest-Host Complexes
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular chemistry offers powerful tools for drug formulation and controlled release, with cyclodextrins (CDs) widely used as drug carriers. However, fine-tuning the binding kinetics and affinity of a drug-CD complex remains a challenge. A novel strategy is needed to externally modulate the CD's binding pocket without direct chemical modification, enabling precise control over drug association and dissociation rates for therapeutic applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Encapsulating a cyclodextrin within a larger, engineered synthetic host (e.g., cucurbit[7]uril or a pillar[6]arene) will insulate and pre-organize the CD, fundamentally altering the thermodynamics and kinetics of drug binding within the CD cavity compared to the free CD.
proposals.method: We will employ the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology to map the binding hotspots of the outer host for CD inclusion. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the CHARMM36 force field, with SILCS-based parameters for host-guest systems, will characterize the conformational landscape and dynamics of free β-CD and the CD-in-host complex. Binding free energies (ΔG) and kinetics (kon, koff) for a model drug (adamantan-1-amine) will be calculated via umbrella sampling and Markov State Models, respectively, with results validated by isothermal titration calorimetry and stopped-flow kinetic assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new computational and experimental framework for designing functional supramolecular relay systems. It will provide quantitative, mechanistic insights into how hierarchical host-guest encapsulation can be used as a general strategy to modulate molecular recognition, directly contributing to the rational design of next-generation drug delivery systems with tailored release profiles.
proposals.novelty: While host-guest chemistry is well-studied, the concept of using a host-encapsulated CD as a functional relay to control the binding of a third molecule is unexplored. This moves beyond structural encapsulation to create a supramolecular assembly with emergent, tunable binding properties.

proposals.title: Enhancing Allosteric Site and Functional State Prediction via SILCS-MD Hybrid Simulations with Adaptive Solvent Representation
proposals.motivation: Accurate prediction of protein-ligand interactions, especially for allosteric sites and functionally relevant conformational states, remains a major challenge in computational drug discovery. Pre-2026 methods, including the researcher's own SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform, excel at mapping local binding hotspots but can be limited by static or semi-static protein frameworks, potentially missing dynamics-driven binding opportunities and the influence of solvent reorganization on binding thermodynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating explicit-solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, guided by SILCS-derived fragment affinity maps, into an adaptive workflow will significantly improve the identification of cryptic allosteric sites and the prediction of ligand-induced functional states by explicitly capturing coupled protein-solvent-ligand dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid SILCS-MD protocol. First, SILCS will be used to generate comprehensive fragment affinity (FragMaps) and solvation (Grid Free Energy) maps for a target protein's known conformational ensemble. These maps will then seed and bias subsequent explicit-solvent MD simulations using the CHARMM36 force field, focusing sampling on regions of high fragment affinity. An adaptive solvent representation will be employed, toggling between SILCS's implicit solvent for rapid mapping and explicit solvent for detailed dynamics. Machine learning (specifically, graph neural networks) will analyze the resulting trajectories to classify protein microstates and predict ligand-binding poses and affinities for novel compounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, publicly available computational pipeline that bridges high-throughput fragment mapping with atomic-resolution dynamics, directly extending the SILCS platform. It will provide validated, dynamic models for challenging drug targets with known allosteric regulation, offering new strategies for functional modulator design. The method will advance the field by providing a more physically rigorous framework for predicting biological activities rooted in protein dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight, iterative coupling of SILCS-based chemical potential calculations with explicit-solvent MD to guide sampling, moving beyond using SILCS as a purely post-MD analysis tool. The adaptive solvent model is a unique technical innovation aimed at balancing efficiency and accuracy for large-scale binding site discovery.

proposals.title: Predicting Dendrimer-Protein Interaction Patterns Using SILCS-Guided Graph Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: Dendrimers are promising nanocarriers for drug delivery and therapeutic agents, but their rational design is hampered by a lack of atomic-level understanding of their complex, multivalent interactions with biological targets. Pre-2026 computational methods like Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) can map interaction fields but struggle to efficiently predict binding patterns for large, flexible dendrimers across diverse protein surfaces. A machine learning framework that learns from SILCS simulations could accelerate the design of functionalized dendrimers.
proposals.hypothesis: That a graph neural network (GNN) trained on SILCS-derived interaction maps and molecular graphs can accurately and rapidly predict the preferred binding modes and interaction hotspots of dendrimers with target proteins.
proposals.method: We will generate a training dataset by performing SILCS simulations for a diverse set of protein targets and small fragment probes. For each protein, we will compute SILCS FragMaps (free energy grids). Dendrimer structures will be represented as molecular graphs. We will train a multi-head graph attention network (GAT) to learn the mapping from the combined input of protein FragMaps (encoded as voxelized 3D tensors) and the dendrimer graph to a predicted interaction pattern, output as a spatial probability density of dendrimer scaffold atoms. The model will be validated against explicit SILCS-MC (Monte Carlo) simulations of full dendrimers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, fast computational tool for predicting dendrimer-biomolecule interactions, directly extending the SILCS methodology. It will provide fundamental insights into the multivalent binding principles of dendritic polymers and establish a pipeline for the structure-based design of dendrimers with tailored protein affinity and selectivity.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS and ML have been used for small molecules, this integrates GNNs with SILCS FragMaps specifically for the high-valency, flexible dendrimer problem, moving beyond static site prediction to dynamic interaction pattern forecasting.

proposals.title: Quantum-Accurate Binding Site Characterization in Dendrimer-Ligand Systems via SILCS-Based QM/MM Integration
proposals.motivation: Dendrimers are promising drug delivery vehicles and antimicrobial agents, but their rational design is hampered by a lack of atomic-level understanding of guest molecule binding. Classical force fields, like those in the established SILCS (Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) platform, can struggle to accurately model key interactions such as cation-π, halogen bonding, and polarization effects at dendrimer binding sites, limiting predictive accuracy for binding affinity and specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating quantum mechanical (QM) calculations with the SILCS methodology will provide a more accurate electronic description of dendrimer binding sites, leading to superior prediction of ligand poses and binding free energies compared to classical SILCS alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid QM/MM-SILCS workflow. First, SILCS simulations with the CHARMM36 force field will identify putative binding sites on model PAMAM dendrimers. For key sites, we will perform single-point energy calculations using Density Functional Theory (DFT, e.g., ωB97X-D/6-31G*) on QM regions encompassing the binding pocket and a probe ligand, embedded in a MM environment. This data will be used to refine the classical free energy fields (FragMaps) and to parameterize specific, polarizable corrections for the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) to better model electronic effects in subsequent SILCS-MC docking and free energy perturbation calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will extend the SILCS platform to enable quantum-informed characterization of soft-material binding sites, providing a novel computational tool for dendrimer-based drug design. It will yield fundamental insights into the electronic determinants of dendrimer-ligand recognition, moving beyond steric and simple electrostatic descriptions.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been integrated with covalent docking and biologics design, its fusion with QM methods for explicit electronic structure refinement of flexible, non-protein binding sites in dendrimers is a novel and non-trivial advancement beyond its current biological applications.

proposals.title: Comprehensive Validation of the SILCS Atom Typer Across Diverse Chemical Space for Enhanced Force Field Parameterization and Ligand Design
proposals.motivation: The accuracy of the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, a cornerstone for computational drug design and protein-ligand binding prediction, is fundamentally dependent on the quality of its underlying atom typing scheme. The initial atom typer was trained and tested on a limited set of 603 compounds, potentially restricting its generalizability to the vast chemical space relevant for drug discovery, including macrocycles, covalent inhibitors, and biologics. A systematic validation on a broader, more diverse molecular set is therefore critical to ensure the robustness of SILCS-based simulations and free energy calculations for novel therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the current SILCS atom typer, while accurate for its initial training set, will exhibit systematic inaccuracies for specific under-represented chemical functionalities (e.g., boron-containing groups, novel warheads, complex stereochemistry), and that identifying and correcting these limitations will significantly improve the accuracy of SILCS-derived binding affinities and hotspot maps for a wider range of drug-like molecules.
proposals.method: We will curate a large, diverse validation library of 2,000+ drug-like molecules from public databases (e.g., ChEMBL, ZINC), explicitly enriching for chemical moieties underrepresented in the original set, such as organometallics, peptidomimetics, and fragments with unusual hybridization states. Each molecule will be processed through the established SILCS-Biologics atom-typing workflow. Accuracy will be assessed by comparing typer-assigned atom types against expert manual curation, quantifying error rates by chemical class. We will then employ an iterative refinement protocol, using the SILCS competitive saturation methodology itself to identify problematic regions of chemical space and guide the expansion or adjustment of the atom type definitions and associated parameters within the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) framework.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorously validated and more chemically robust atom-typing tool for the SILCS platform, directly increasing the reliability of its applications in cryptic site identification, covalent drug design, and biologics modeling. The resulting publicly available dataset of typed molecules will serve as a benchmark for future force field development. Furthermore, the identified limitations and correction strategies will inform best practices for applying SILCS to novel chemical entities.
proposals.novelty: While atom typing is a classic problem, this study uniquely leverages the SILCS simulation framework not just as a consumer of atom types, but as an active feedback mechanism to diagnose and refine them, creating a closed-loop between method validation and force field parameterization specifically for the demanding context of biomolecular competitive saturation.

proposals.title: Optimization of the SILCS Atom Typer for Ultra-High-Throughput Virtual Screening of Millions of Compounds
proposals.motivation: The Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) framework is a powerful tool for binding site identification, protein charge prediction, and covalent drug design. However, its application in early-stage virtual screening (VS) of ultra-large chemical libraries (e.g., Enamine REAL, ZINC20), containing tens to hundreds of millions of molecules, is bottlenecked by the computational cost of the atom typing step. This step, which assigns pharmacophore types for FragMaps generation and ligand-grid free energy calculations, must be drastically accelerated to integrate SILCS into modern, scalable VS pipelines.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the SILCS atom typing algorithm can be fundamentally re-engineered using optimized data structures and parallel computing paradigms to achieve orders-of-magnitude speedup without loss of accuracy, enabling the routine screening of multi-million-molecule libraries.
proposals.method: We will implement a multi-tiered optimization strategy. First, we will replace iterative, serial atom-type assignment loops with vectorized operations using NumPy/SciPy. Second, we will develop a GPU-accelerated kernel using CUDA or OpenCL for the most computationally intensive steps, such as spatial search and electron density field calculations. Third, we will integrate a fast, in-memory molecular cache and pre-computed parameter tables to minimize redundant I/O and disk access. The optimized typer will be validated against the standard SILCS typer on benchmark datasets (e.g., PDBbind) for accuracy and benchmarked for speed on libraries of increasing size up to 10 million molecules.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly enable ultra-high-throughput virtual screening within the SILCS platform, dramatically expanding its utility in early drug discovery. It will provide the community with a production-ready, open-source tool for rapid SILCS-based pharmacophore analysis. The technical framework will also serve as a blueprint for accelerating other force-field parameter assignment tasks in computational biophysics.
proposals.novelty: While general molecular docking speedups exist, this work represents the first dedicated, low-level optimization of the core SILCS atom typer, a unique and critical component for SILCS-based screening. The integration of GPU acceleration specifically for the SILCS fragmentation and typing process is a novel computational strategy.

proposals.title: Automated Validation of Molecular Connectivity and Protonation States via Self-Consistency of CHARMM General Force Field Atom Type Assignments
proposals.motivation: In computational drug design, the accuracy of molecular mechanics simulations depends critically on correct input molecular structures, including bond connectivity, atom hybridization, and protonation states. Manual curation is error-prone, and existing validation tools (e.g., basic stereochemistry checks) often miss subtle errors that lead to incorrect atom typing and subsequent flawed binding free energy predictions or ligand docking poses. An automated, physics-based validation method is needed to improve reliability in structure-based design workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inconsistencies in the assignment of CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) atom types, derived from the molecular connectivity and chemical environment, can serve as a robust, automated indicator of errors in input molecular structure files.
proposals.method: We will develop a software tool that integrates the established Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology for molecular fragmentation and CGenFF atom type assignment. The tool will parse an input molecular structure, perform CGenFF atom typing, and then apply a rule-based inference engine to flag inconsistencies. For example, an sp2 carbon assigned an alkane carbon type, or a hydrogen bond donor atom lacking a polar hydrogen, would trigger an error. The algorithm will be validated against known benchmark sets of correct and intentionally corrupted structures from drug-like libraries and SILCS-Biologics protein-ligand complexes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, integrated validation module for the SILCS platform, enhancing the robustness of covalent and non-covalent drug design workflows. It will provide researchers with an automated, chemically intelligent pre-simulation check, reducing human error and computational waste. The method will also contribute to improving the accuracy of protein-ligand binding predictions by ensuring reliable initial structures.
proposals.novelty: While atom typing is a standard step in force field parameterization, its systematic use as a reverse diagnostic for molecular connectivity errors is unexplored. This approach leverages the deep, pre-existing chemical knowledge encoded in the CGenFF rules and SILCS framework to create a validation tool, moving beyond simple geometric checks to environment-aware chemical plausibility testing.

proposals.title: Evaluating Implicit and Hybrid Solvation Models for SILCS-Based Ligand Design: Accuracy and Efficiency in Binding Site Identification and Free Energy Prediction
proposals.motivation: Explicit solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations, as used in the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform, provide high accuracy for mapping protein-ligand interactions but are computationally expensive. This limits high-throughput applications in drug discovery. Implicit solvent models offer significant speed advantages, but their accuracy for the detailed, heterogeneous binding environments probed by SILCS remains inadequately tested, particularly for challenging targets like membrane proteins or allosteric sites.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid solvation approach, combining implicit solvent for bulk regions with explicit solvent in the binding site, or a well-parameterized implicit solvent model alone, can reproduce the binding site FragMaps and ligand binding free energies from full explicit solvent SILCS simulations with equivalent accuracy at a substantially reduced computational cost.
proposals.method: We will implement and benchmark the Generalized Born (GB) model with the Onufriev-Bashford-Case (OBC) parameters and a hybrid explicit/implicit solvent scheme within the CHARMM simulation package. Using established SILCS workflows, we will compute FragMaps for a diverse test set of proteins, including soluble enzymes (e.g., ER α-glucosidase I) and a G-protein coupled receptor (GPCR). We will compare these maps to our gold-standard explicit solvent SILCS results using spatial overlap metrics and by predicting binding poses and affinities for known ligands, validating against experimental data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, quantitative assessment of solvation model efficiency for SILCS, potentially enabling faster, large-scale virtual screening and covalent drug design workflows. It will deliver optimized protocols and parameter recommendations for using implicit/hybrid models in structure-based design, broadening the accessibility of the SILCS methodology.
proposals.novelty: While implicit solvent models are widely used, their systematic validation within the specific context of SILCS—which relies on the statistical distribution of small molecule probes—is novel. The direct comparison of explicit, implicit, and hybrid models for generating functional binding site maps for drug design addresses a critical, unresolved question in computational biophysics.

proposals.title: Systematic Assessment of Computational Binding Affinity Prediction Robustness to Experimental Data Variability Using SILCS
proposals.motivation: Computational drug design critically depends on accurate experimental binding affinity data for method validation and parameterization. However, pre-2026 literature reveals significant variability in reported affinities for the same protein-ligand complex across different experimental techniques (e.g., ITC vs. SPR) and laboratories, leading to disputed values. This variability introduces uncertainty into the development and benchmarking of force fields and binding free energy methods, potentially compromising their predictive reliability in prospective design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the robustness and predictive accuracy of the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) platform for binding affinity prediction and site identification are significantly affected by the choice of single, potentially non-representative, experimental affinity values used for calibration and validation.
proposals.method: We will curate a benchmark set of protein-ligand complexes with multiple, and sometimes conflicting, experimental binding affinity measurements from disparate sources (e.g., ChEMBL, BindingDB). Using the SILCS methodology, we will perform binding free energy calculations (SILCS-BFE) and functional group binding site mapping (SILCS-MC) for each complex. We will systematically assess the correlation of SILCS predictions against different experimental data subsets, quantify the sensitivity of the SILCS FragMaps and GFE scores to the input calibration data, and implement a Bayesian weighting scheme within the SILCS framework to account for experimental uncertainty and derive consensus affinity estimates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating computational method robustness in the face of real-world experimental data variability. It will yield best-practice guidelines for data curation in force field validation and produce a publicly available, annotated dataset of complexes with affinity discrepancies, serving as a critical stress-test benchmark for the computational chemistry community.
proposals.novelty: While SILCS has been extensively validated, its systematic evaluation against disputed or heterogeneous experimental data is novel. This approach moves beyond single-value benchmarks to explicitly model and mitigate the impact of experimental uncertainty on computational predictions, a critical but often overlooked step in method development.

proposals.title: Inverse Free Energy Perturbation Guided by SILCS: A Computational Workflow for Ligand Affinity Optimization
proposals.motivation: Free Energy Perturbation (FEP) is a gold-standard method for predicting relative binding free energies (ΔΔG) between congeneric ligands, but its use is inherently retrospective, analyzing pre-defined modifications. A method to inversely propose specific ligand modifications that yield a desired ΔΔG improvement would transform structure-based drug design from an iterative screening process into a more directed engineering task. This addresses a critical bottleneck in lead optimization, where identifying the right chemical changes to improve affinity remains challenging.
proposals.hypothesis: By inverting the FEP framework and integrating it with the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology, we can systematically generate and rank ligand modifications that are predicted to optimize binding affinity for a given target protein.
proposals.method: We will develop a workflow where, for a given protein-ligand complex, the SILCS FragMaps (density fields of functional group interactions) will be used to propose chemically sensible modifications at designated R-group sites. An inverse FEP algorithm will then treat these proposed modifications as alchemical perturbations and solve for the relative free energy change, effectively back-calculating the chemical features required to achieve a target ΔΔG. The core computational engine will leverage a hybrid of Monte Carlo sampling for modification generation and analytical gradient-based optimization within the FEP potential of mean force framework, all parameterized using the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) developed by the Mackerell lab.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a novel, automated computational tool for prospective ligand affinity optimization, directly outputting synthetically accessible chemical modifications with predicted ΔΔG values. It will extend the utility of SILCS from binding site analysis and charge prediction to active design, and provide a rigorous, physics-based alternative to purely machine-learning-driven generative models for small molecule design.
proposals.novelty: While FEP is widely used for validation, its formal inversion to propose optimal perturbations is a non-trivial and underexplored problem. The tight integration of this inverse-FEP approach with the spatially resolved, functional-group-centric data from SILCS FragMaps creates a unique, knowledge-driven design pipeline distinct from existing methods.

proposals.title: Automated Parametrization of Novel Ligands for the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) via SILCS-Based Fragment Analysis
proposals.motivation: The CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF) is essential for accurate molecular dynamics simulations of drug-like molecules, but manual parametrization of new chemical entities is a time-consuming, expert-driven bottleneck. This hinders high-throughput computational drug discovery and the study of novel scaffolds, particularly in emerging therapeutic areas. An automated, robust tool is needed to democratize access to high-quality force field parameters and accelerate structure-based design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the manual, knowledge-based parametrization workflow for CGenFF can be automated by leveraging the Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) methodology to derive initial parameters from fragment binding free energies and subsequently refining them using a rule-based system informed by the CGenFF parametrization philosophy.
proposals.method: The software will implement a multi-stage pipeline. First, the target molecule will be fragmented using rules derived from the CGenFF topology and parameter hierarchy. Key fragments will be simulated within the SILCS framework to obtain fragment binding free energy maps in a model solvent, providing initial estimates for partial atomic charges and Lennard-Jones parameters. These will be integrated and refined by a rule-based engine that applies CGenFF's standard optimization targets (e.g., reproducing quantum mechanical interaction energies and geometries) and penalty scores, automating the decision logic currently performed manually by experts. The final output will be formatted CGenFF stream files ready for simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly available, automated parametrization tool directly aligned with the CGenFF philosophy, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for non-expert users. It will enhance the throughput of SILCS-based drug design campaigns by enabling rapid parametrization of newly designed compounds. The tool will be validated against a benchmark set of manually parametrized drug molecules from prior studies.
proposals.novelty: While other force field parametrization tools exist (e.g., Antechamber for GAFF), this approach is novel in its direct integration of SILCS-derived fragment energetics to inform parameter assignment and its explicit encoding of the CGenFF-specific parametrization rules and optimization criteria into an automated workflow.

proposals.title: Mapping and Prioritizing Chemical Space for Force Field Development Using SILCS-Based Cheminformatics
proposals.motivation: The accuracy of molecular mechanics force fields, such as the CHARMM General Force Field (CGenFF), is critical for reliable structure-based drug design and binding affinity prediction. However, force field parameterization is resource-intensive and often lags behind the exploration of novel chemical scaffolds in drug discovery. A systematic, data-driven method to identify and prioritize under-parameterized regions of chemical space is needed to guide efficient force field development.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating cheminformatics analyses with Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation (SILCS) simulations, we can quantitatively map the chemical space currently well-sampled by existing force fields and identify high-priority gaps corresponding to pharmacologically relevant but poorly parameterized fragments.
proposals.method: We will extract molecular fragments from large, diverse drug-like libraries (e.g., ChEMBL, ZINC) and compute cheminformatic descriptors (e.g., Morgan fingerprints, BCUT metrics, topological polar surface area). These fragments will be cross-referenced with the CGenFF parameter library. SILCS simulations will be performed on benchmark protein targets to generate FragMaps, and the functional group distributions from the simulations will be compared to the cheminformatics-derived chemical space. Gaps will be prioritized using a scoring function combining fragment prevalence in drug databases, synthetic accessibility, and the magnitude of free energy discrepancies observed in SILCS for similar fragments.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available, ranked list of chemical fragments for prioritized force field parameterization, directly linking cheminformatics to simulation needs. It will establish a novel workflow that uses SILCS not just for binding site analysis, but as a diagnostic tool for force field coverage, thereby accelerating the development of more accurate and broadly applicable biomolecular force fields.
proposals.novelty: While cheminformatics is commonly used for library analysis, its integration with SILCS-based functional group sampling to audit and guide force field development is a novel, synergistic approach. This moves beyond retrospective validation to proactive, simulation-informed prioritization of parameterization efforts.

ideas.title: NMR-Guided Coarse-Graining for Organelle-Scale Membrane Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates solution-state NMR spectroscopy directly into the development and validation of multi-scale molecular models for complex organelle membranes. Experimental NMR data on the dynamics of specific host-guest complexes (e.g., lipid-protein, lipid-drug) within native-like membrane mimetics is used to parameterize and rigorously test a coarse-grained force field. This validated force field is then employed to simulate large patches of heterogeneous organelle membranes, such as those of the endoplasmic reticulum or inner mitochondrial membrane. The synergy lies in using the NMR-derived dynamic constraints to ensure that the simplified large-scale simulations retain mechanistic accuracy for critical molecular recognition and remodeling events that govern organelle function.

ideas.title: Validate complex stability using a multi-probe experimental suite from structural biology
ideas.description: This idea proposes to validate theoretical stability estimates for molecular complexes by adapting the multi-probe validation approach common in structural biology. The analogous problem of confirming protein-ligand binding stability is solved by using a complementary suite of techniques (ITC, SPR, crystallography) to provide convergent evidence. Adapted to this context, the research would employ a designed suite of experiments—ITC for thermodynamics, AUC for solution-state assembly, and time-resolved fluorescence for kinetics—to rigorously test and confirm the predicted stability and structure of complexes across different stoichiometries, creating a robust, multi-faceted experimental validation.

ideas.title: Inverse Stability Design via Computational Protein Engineering Analogy
ideas.description: This idea inverts the discovery process by computationally designing guest molecules to achieve a desired complex stability, drawing a direct analogy from computational protein engineering. In that field, inverse design algorithms (e.g., Rosetta) start with a target protein fold and function, then search sequence space to find amino acid sequences predicted to achieve it. Adapted here, the process begins with a defined host structure and a target binding free energy (stability). A computational framework will then search and assemble virtual guest molecules from building blocks, using scoring functions based on molecular mechanics and machine learning to predict host-guest interaction energy and synthetic feasibility. The output is a set of novel, synthetically accessible guest structures computationally predicted to form complexes with the precisely desired stability.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Folding Models to Melt Complexation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that complexation mechanisms are directly comparable between melt and aqueous phases by drawing an analogy from computational biochemistry. In protein folding, researchers moved beyond simple aqueous comparisons by developing models parameterized for the specific crowded cellular environment. Adapting this solution, the research would treat silicate melts or other high-temperature melts not as 'hot water' but as a unique solvent with emergent properties. A new simulation or experimental framework would be developed to predict metal-ligand complexation in melts based on fundamental melt properties (e.g., optical basicity, network polymerization, cation field strength), explicitly decoupling from aqueous-phase analogies and deriving melt-specific stability 'constants' or mechanistic pathways.

ideas.title: Mapping the Stoichiometric Landscape: A Phase Diagram Approach to Complex Assembly
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single dominant stoichiometry exists for each complex under given conditions. Drawing an analogy from materials science, where phase diagrams define the stability regions of different solid compositions, we propose adapting this framework to molecular complexes. The analogous solution involves mapping stability based on external parameters. The adapted research idea is to construct 'stoichiometric phase diagrams' for model protein complexes. By systematically varying conditions like subunit concentration, buffer composition, and temperature, and using techniques like native mass spectrometry or analytical ultracentrifugation to quantify populations, we can define the boundaries where one stoichiometric form is favored, where another takes over, and where dynamic mixtures coexist. This shifts the paradigm from seeking a single 'correct' stoichiometry to understanding the multidimensional landscape that governs complex assembly.

ideas.title: Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification for Phase-Dependent Force Field Transferability
ideas.description: This research direction applies Bayesian inference to systematically evaluate the assumption that molecular force field parameters, particularly for lipids or other self-assembling systems, are directly transferable between aqueous solutions and molten or dehydrated phases. Instead of a simple binary comparison, the Bayesian framework quantifies the posterior uncertainty and potential bias in parameters when fitted to data from one phase and used to predict behavior in the other. This yields a probabilistic measure of model transferability, identifying which specific interaction terms (e.g., tail-tail, head-group, or ion binding parameters) are most sensitive to the phase environment. The result is a principled, uncertainty-aware methodology for developing phase-robust force fields or for diagnosing when distinct parameter sets are fundamentally required.

ideas.title: Employ NMR Relaxation Dispersion to Map Supramolecular Exchange Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution-state NMR relaxation dispersion techniques, specifically CPMG and R1ρ methods, from the field of protein folding and conformational dynamics to the study of supramolecular host-guest complexes. In structural biology, these experiments are the gold standard for probing 'invisible' excited states and quantifying micro-to-millisecond exchange processes. The adapted research idea applies this methodology to supramolecular systems to experimentally probe and quantify dynamic events like guest exchange, translocation within hosts, or host adaptation. This will move beyond simple structural validation to provide a detailed, quantitative map of the kinetic and thermodynamic landscape governing supramolecular dynamics and complexation.

ideas.title: Apply Bayesian Optimization for Force Field Parameter Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Bayesian optimization from the field of experimental design and machine learning hyperparameter tuning to the problem of force field parameter refinement. In the analogous field, Bayesian optimization efficiently minimizes a costly objective (like model validation error) by building a surrogate model to guide a minimal number of evaluations. Adapted here, the method treats the force field's inaccuracy against diverse target data (QM energies, experimental properties) as the expensive objective, where each evaluation requires running molecular dynamics or Monte Carlo simulations. A Gaussian Process or similar surrogate model will be trained on prior parameter-performance pairs. It will then propose the most promising new parameter set to simulate next, balancing the need to explore broad parameter space and exploit regions of high accuracy. This creates an iterative, closed-loop optimization that refines force field parameters with significantly greater efficiency than traditional manual or brute-force approaches, systematically improving agreement with the target data.

ideas.title: Functional Relay Complexes: Cyclodextrin as an Insulated Guest within Engineered Hosts to Modulate Drug Binding Kinetics
ideas.description: This research investigates cyclodextrin (CD) acting as a guest within a larger, engineered synthetic host (e.g., cucurbituril, pillararene, or designed nanocage) not as an end in itself, but to create a functional supramolecular assembly. The core hypothesis is that the outer host will insulate and pre-organize the CD, fundamentally altering its ability to bind a third molecule, such as a drug or catalyst, within its own cavity. The study will quantitatively compare the binding affinity, kinetics, and selectivity of a model drug (e.g., adamantane or a small steroid) to free CD versus the CD-in-host complex, testing for emergent relay properties. This transforms the concept from structural curiosity to a platform for designing controlled-release systems or modulated catalysts.

ideas.title: A Multi-Technique Pipeline Combining Paramagnetic and Photo-CIDNP NMR with Single-Molecule Probes to Map Host-Guest Energy Landscapes
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the averaging limitations of conventional NMR by employing a suite of specialized NMR techniques alongside single-molecule methods. Paramagnetic NMR and photo-CIDNP are used to detect, populate, and characterize sparsely populated or transient states within host-guest complexes. These experimental observations provide explicit restraints for molecular dynamics simulations to model the complete free energy landscape. Finally, single-molecule force spectroscopy techniques directly measure the binding forces and kinetic transitions, providing independent validation of the dynamic pathways and energetics inferred from the integrated NMR and simulation data.

ideas.title: Adaptive Refinement of Force Fields via Active Learning and Transfer from High-Fidelity Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hybrid modeling strategy from Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), specifically RANS-LES methods, to the problem of force field refinement for host-guest interactions. In CFD, a computationally efficient model (RANS) is used for most of a flow domain, while a high-fidelity model (LES) is deployed adaptively in critical regions guided by an error sensor. The analogous solution here is to create an adaptive simulation framework for molecular systems. A standard classical force field would provide baseline dynamics, but a real-time error estimator (based on geometric descriptors or local electronic structure indicators) would identify configurations where host-guest interactions are poorly described. For these critical configurations, a high-accuracy ML potential, trained on quantum mechanical (QM) data, would be invoked to compute the correct interaction energy and forces. These targeted ML calculations would not only provide accurate energies for the simulation at hand but would also generate a focused dataset to iteratively refine and correct the parameters of the classical force field specifically for the problematic interaction types, leading to a force field that becomes more accurate and reliable through use, without the constant need for full QM reference calculations.

ideas.title: Bayesian inference of dynamic stoichiometry ensembles in lipid complexes
ideas.description: This research direction applies Bayesian inference to calibrate lipid force field parameters against experimental data, explicitly treating the existence of multiple, dynamically interconverting stoichiometries for a given complex as a model hypothesis. The posterior parameter distributions are then used to predict not a single dominant structure, but a probabilistic ensemble of coexisting stoichiometries and their relative populations under specific thermodynamic conditions. This approach quantifies the uncertainty in both the parameters and the predicted stoichiometric states, revealing whether observed data decisively supports a single stable form or necessitates a mixture. The outcome is a statistically rigorous framework that can identify conditions where stoichiometric plasticity is an inherent, measurable feature of the system.

ideas.title: Primary Hydrophobic Binding via Shape-Complementary Cavity Occupation in Apolar Solvents
ideas.description: This redesigned study rigorously tests if hydrophobic forces can be the primary and specific driver of molecular recognition by eliminating all major competing interactions. It employs a synthetic host with a well-defined, rigid, and non-polar cavity paired with a series of purely aliphatic hydrocarbon guests that vary in shape and branching. Binding affinities are measured in a non-polar, non-competitive solvent environment where the driving force is the release of poorly solvated guests into the cavity and the optimization of van der Waals contacts. This system directly probes whether subtle differences in hydrophobic surface complementarity and packing efficiency can dictate strong, selective binding in the absence of any other directional interactions.

ideas.title: Multi-Fidelity, Uncertainty-Driven ML Refinement of Force Fields for Host-Guest Binding
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the limitation of training on potentially biased QM data by employing a multi-fidelity machine learning strategy. The ML potential is trained on a carefully curated ensemble of quantum chemical data, where contributions from different theory levels are weighted based on their agreement with a small set of high-accuracy benchmarks or experimental binding data for prototypical host-guest pairs. An integrated uncertainty quantification engine actively identifies host-guest conformations where the model's prediction confidence is low, guiding adaptive sampling and on-the-fly high-level QM calculations to fill knowledge gaps. This creates a self-correcting, iterative refinement loop that produces a force field correction which is both chemically accurate and aware of its own limitations.

ideas.title: Dual: optimize for kinetics over structure
ideas.description: Re-parameterize the force field to match dynamic transition rates instead of equilibrium structures.

ideas.title: Experimental Validation of Inverted Force Fields for Complex Stability
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the inverse scattering approach with multi-technique experimental validation. First, X-ray and neutron scattering data are inverted to directly infer force field parameters for molecular complexes. These derived force fields are then used to predict stability and structure for various stoichiometries. Crucially, these predictions are tested and refined using a suite of orthogonal experimental techniques (e.g., calorimetry, spectroscopy, analytical ultracentrifugation), creating a feedback loop that validates the inverted parameters and improves the accuracy of the inversion method itself for complex, multi-component systems.

ideas.title: Closed-loop adaptive force field refinement driven by predictive error modeling under ionic stress
ideas.description: This system moves beyond passive validation to actively predict and correct force field failures under varied ionic conditions. A machine learning model is trained on a curated dataset of simulation errors (vs. experimental or high-level reference data) across a broad spectrum of salt types and concentrations. The model then predicts regions of parameter space where the force field will likely fail, directing computational resources to perform targeted quantum mechanical calculations on those specific ion-pair or ion-solute interactions. The resulting parameters are iteratively fed back into the force field, creating a self-improving loop that systematically enhances robustness and predictive accuracy for novel electrolyte solutions.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Membrane-Embedded QM/MM
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of Adaptive Mesh Refinement from Computational Fluid Dynamics to the challenge of embedding QM regions within lipid bilayers. In CFD, AMR dynamically increases grid resolution only in critical regions like shock fronts. Translated to QM/MM, this means developing a simulation framework where the high-cost quantum mechanical region is not static but dynamically reshapes and resizes in response to the evolving reaction at the membrane interface. An algorithm would monitor electronic density, bond formation/breaking, or charge transfer, triggering the expansion of the QM region to encompass newly involved lipid headgroups, water molecules, or cofactors, and its contraction as the reaction localizes. This adaptive scaling ensures computational resources are focused precisely where quantum accuracy is essential, enabling the study of complex, multi-stage chemical reactions that propagate across the membrane-solvent interface without the prohibitive cost of a static, oversized QM region.

ideas.title: Quantifying Ligand-Specific Divergence: Stability Constants of Zr-F Complexation in Silicate Melt vs. Aqueous Fluid
ideas.description: This research directly tests the assumption of mechanistic comparability by quantifying and contrasting the stability constants and coordination geometries of a specific metal-ligand complex (Zr⁴⁺-F⁻) across silicate melt and aqueous phases. Experiments will employ in-situ spectroscopic techniques on compositionally controlled systems at geologically relevant temperatures and pressures. By measuring how complex stability varies with ligand activity in each medium, the work will generate concrete, quantitative data to identify where and why melt and aqueous complexation mechanisms fundamentally diverge. The outcome will be a calibrated, ligand-specific framework for predicting when aqueous models fail for melt systems.

ideas.title: Calibrating Membrane Simulations with a Fluorescence Correlation Spectroscopy (FCS) Benchmarking Framework
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the problem of quantitatively comparing lipid diffusion simulation results to experiment by drawing an analogy from astrophysics. In cosmology, complex simulations are validated against a standardized set of observational benchmarks. Adapted to membrane biophysics, the solution is to create a formal benchmarking framework. This involves curating a standardized suite of high-quality experimental lipid lateral diffusion data (from NMR, FCS) for canonical membrane systems. The core adapted idea is to develop and promote community-agreed protocols for calculating diffusion coefficients from simulations (handling finite-size effects, analysis time scales) and defined metrics for comparison, enabling objective, quantitative scoring of simulation methodologies against experimental reality, thereby driving force field and method improvement.

ideas.title: Bayesian Uncertainty-Quantified Force Field Refinement via Scattering Data and Sparse QM Anchors
ideas.description: This approach refines classical force field parameters by integrating experimental X-ray/neutron scattering data within a Bayesian inference framework, directly addressing the ill-posedness of the inverse problem. Instead of seeking a single 'inverted' parameter set, the method computes a full posterior distribution, explicitly revealing parameter uncertainties and correlations. The scattering likelihood is combined with sparse, high-fidelity QM calculations on critical molecular fragments to anchor energetically sensitive parameters. The final output is not just an improved parameter set, but a statistically rigorous understanding of what the scattering data can and cannot determine, enabling robust, uncertainty-aware molecular simulations.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Hydrophobic 'Background Signal' in Hydrogen-Bonded Complexes Using a Noise-Separation Approach
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from signal processing to quantify the secondary role of hydrophobic interactions in molecular complexes where hydrogen bonding is present. In communications engineering, techniques like matched filtering and statistical decomposition are used to isolate a weak signal from a stronger background. By analogously treating the total binding free energy as a composite signal, we can design an experimental and computational framework. This involves creating systematic perturbations to molecular structures (e.g., via mutagenesis or synthetic chemistry) to modulate hydrogen-bonding strength independently of hydrophobic surface area. Applying statistical energy decomposition or isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) data analysis inspired by background subtraction methods will allow us to isolate and precisely quantify the hydrophobic 'background signal' contribution, revealing its role even when it is energetically masked by dominant hydrogen bonds.

ideas.title: Background-Free Single-Molecule Binding Force Spectroscopy via Nanoconfinement
ideas.description: This redesigned approach combines optical tweezers or AFM with zero-mode waveguide (ZMW) nanostructures to perform force spectroscopy under extreme spatial confinement. The host molecule is tethered to the bottom of a metallic ZMW, and the guest is attached to the force probe, ensuring their interaction occurs within the sub-attoliter volume where the optical field or mechanical probe is active. This physical exclusion of the bulk environment eliminates virtually all non-specific interactions and background signal. The result is a direct, unambiguous measurement of the specific host-guest binding force, free from the artifacts that plague conventional single-molecule measurements on surfaces.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Active Learning for Physically Plausible Force Field Refinement
ideas.description: This approach uses machine learning to refine force field parameters against diverse target data, but crucially embeds hard and soft physical constraints directly into the optimization process to ensure the resulting parameters are physically meaningful. The system employs a hybrid loss function that combines data error with penalties for violating physical laws and prior knowledge, such as energy conservation and correct asymptotic behavior. An active learning loop identifies and targets simulations where the current parameters produce physically questionable behavior, ensuring robustness and transferability beyond the initial training set.

ideas.title: Prior-Robust Bayesian Inference with Empirical Priors for Lipid Force Field Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This approach enhances standard Bayesian inference for lipid force field parameters by directly confronting the prior-sensitivity problem. It constructs initial, data-informed prior distributions through systematic simulation of tractable sub-models and fragment molecules, grounding assumptions in computational experiment. The core Bayesian inference is then embedded within a rigorous sensitivity analysis that maps how the posterior parameter distributions and their uncertainties shift across a plausible space of prior specifications. The final output is not just a single posterior distribution, but a robust assessment of which uncertainties are data-constrained and which remain prior-dependent, providing a more honest and actionable quantification of force field reliability.

ideas.title: Inverse Scattering with Bayesian Inference from Seismology
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the Bayesian inverse problem framework from geophysical seismology to molecular scattering. In seismology, researchers infer Earth's internal elastic parameters from surface seismic measurements using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling within a Bayesian framework to handle non-uniqueness and quantify uncertainties. Similarly, we propose to treat X-ray/neutron scattering intensity profiles (I(q) or S(q)) as analogous to seismic waveforms, with molecular force field parameters (bond, angle, dihedral, and non-bonded terms) as the unknown 'subsurface' properties. By implementing a Bayesian MCMC inversion that directly maps scattering data to force field parameter posterior distributions, we can bypass quantum mechanical calculations, inherently quantify parameter uncertainties, and naturally handle the ill-posed nature of the inverse scattering problem. This approach would provide not just optimal parameters but full probability distributions, revealing correlations and degeneracies in parameter space that traditional fitting methods might miss.
